import Mathlib

open Matrix
open scoped BigOperators Matrix.Norms.Frobenius

noncomputable section

abbrev SqMat (d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ
abbrev ColVec (d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin d) (Fin 1) ℝ

def misalignment {d : ℕ} (n : ℕ) (βTT : ColVec d) (W : SqMat d) : ℝ :=
  (((βTT.transpose) * (((1 : SqMat d) - (n : ℝ) • W) * ((1 : SqMat d) - (n : ℝ) • W)) * βTT) 0 0)

def signalPower {d : ℕ} (n : ℕ) (βTT : ColVec d) (W : SqMat d) : ℝ :=
  (n : ℝ) * (‖βTT‖ ^ (2 : ℕ)) * (‖W‖ ^ (2 : ℕ))

metadata.source_paper: Characterizing the Expressivity of Fixed-Precision Transformer Language Models

natural_language.core_claim: Fixed-precision transformer language recognizers with soft attention, strict future masking, and no positional encodings are exactly as expressive as LTL[P].
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the transformer language recognizer architecture defined with a finite floating-point set, soft self-attention, strict future masking, no positional encodings, and acceptance determined by a scalar output at the EOS position. Every such transformer can be simulated by a PFO²[<] formula. Every LTL[P] formula can be simulated by such a transformer. Moreover, PFO²[<] and LTL[P] have the same expressive power.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument has three parts. First, LTL[P] and PFO²[<] are shown equivalent by two translations: translate an LTL[P] formula into PFO²[<] by structural induction, sending the past operator Pψ to an existential quantification over an earlier position; then translate a PFO²[<] formula back into LTL[P] by induction on quantifier depth, sending every subformula of the form ∃y < x : φ(y) to P applied to the translation of φ. Second, every transformer is translated into PFO²[<]. The embedding layer is encoded directly, and the logic is closed under the remaining pointwise transformer operations. The key extra work is soft attention: the exponential map is definable by finite-case enumeration under fixed precision, the softmax denominator is a sum of non-negative fixed-precision terms, and the value aggregation is a weighted sum with only boundedly many non-zero weights because fixed-precision softmax has bounded support. Hence all attention computations are definable in PFO²[<]. Third, every LTL[P] formula is simulated by a transformer. A dedicated hidden-state coordinate is assigned to each subformula, with 1 meaning true and 0 meaning false at a position. Atomic predicates are realized in the embedding layer, Boolean connectives are implemented by feedforward layers, and the past operator is implemented by attention to earlier positions where the subformula already holds. Because fixed precision can make attention weights vanish when too many positions are attended to, the construction adds a second-layer correction that computes P(ψ ∧ Pψ) and combines it with the first attempt; rounding errors are then repaired by thresholding in a feedforward layer; and layer normalization is neutralized by pairing each logical coordinate with a mirror coordinate so that normalization acts as the identity. The classification head finally copies the coordinate corresponding to the target formula.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem cefptlm_582314
    (α : Type u) [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α] [Nonempty α] :
    SameExpressivePower (PFO2LtClass α) (LTLPastClass α) ∧
      (∀ T : FixedPrecisionSoftAttentionStrictFutureMaskedNoPETransformerRecognizer α,
        ∃ φ : PFO2LtFormula α, φ.denotes = T.recognizes) ∧
      (∀ φ : LTLPastFormula α,
        ∃ T : FixedPrecisionSoftAttentionStrictFutureMaskedNoPETransformerRecognizer α,
          T.recognizes = φ.denotes) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: On Learning Verifiers and Implications to Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

natural_language.core_claim: A finite class of trustable stepwise verifiers can be learned with only logarithmic dependence on the class size, provided that training reveals the full gold-standard reasoning traces for each sampled problem.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the stepwise trustably-verifiably PAC learning setting for sequential reasoning. For each sampled problem, the learner is given the full set of gold-standard correct reasoning traces, and a learned verifier is required, on most future problems drawn from the same problem distribution, to accept every gold-standard trace and to reject any reasoning trace as soon as it deviates from every gold-standard trace. Then any finite verifier class H is learnable in this setting with sample complexity (1 / ε) * (log |H| + log (1 / δ)). Equivalently, for any ε, δ in (0, 1), after that many sampled problems, there is a learner whose output, with probability at least 1 - δ, is 1-complete with respect to the gold-standard traces and stepwise sound on at least a 1 - ε fraction of future problems.
natural_language.informal_proof: Use the learner that outputs any verifier in H that is perfectly consistent with the constructed training sample. For each sampled problem, include all gold-standard traces as positive examples, and for every first possible deviation from the gold-standard proof tree include the corresponding deviating prefix as a negative example. Now fix any verifier h in H whose error on future problems is at least ε. Whenever h errs on a sampled problem, one of two things happens: either it accepts some faulty trace, in which case it must accept one of the negative examples corresponding to the first deviation from the gold-standard tree, or it rejects some gold-standard trace, in which case it fails on a positive example. Therefore, on each sampled problem, h avoids detection with probability at most 1 - ε, so across m independent sampled problems it stays consistent with probability at most (1 - ε)^m. A union bound over all h in H shows that the probability that any bad verifier remains consistent is at most |H| * (1 - ε)^m. Choosing m = (1 / ε) * (log |H| + log (1 / δ)) makes this at most δ, so with probability at least 1 - δ every consistent verifier has error below ε, which proves the claim.

formal_language.src_header: import Mathlib

theorem LVICTR_482731_theorem_4_4

    (H : Set (X → List Sym → Bool))
    [Finite ↥H]
    (StepwiseTVPACLearnable :
      Set (X → List Sym → Bool) → (ℝ → ℝ → ℕ) → Prop) :
    StepwiseTVPACLearnable H
      (fun ε δ =>
        Nat.ceil ((1 / ε) * (Real.log (Nat.card ↥H : ℝ) + Real.log (1 / δ)))) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Reasoning by Superposition: A Theoretical Perspective on Chain of Continuous Thought

natural_language.core_claim: A graph-independent two-layer transformer can solve directed graph reachability with chain of continuous thought using any number of reasoning steps larger than the graph diameter.
natural_language.informal_theorem: For every positive Tmax, if the total input length formed by the graph-reachability prompt, C continuous thought vectors, and the special answer token <A> is at most Tmax, then there exists a construction of graph-independent parameters for a two-layer transformer together with a readout matrix, with embedding dimension d growing linearly with the vocabulary size, such that for any directed graph G = (V, E) with at most nmax nodes and nmax smaller than the vocabulary size, any root node r, any two candidate destination nodes c1 and c2 with exactly one of them reachable from r, and any number of continuous-thought steps C larger than the diameter of G, greedy decoding after those C continuous-thought steps outputs the uniquely reachable candidate.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof reuses the same graph-independent transformer construction established for the superposition lemma. First, it shows that after c continuous-thought steps, the hidden vector is exactly the normalized superposition of all vertices reachable from the root within c graph steps. Therefore, when C is larger than the graph diameter, the last continuous thought contains the entire reachable set. Next, the answer token is constructed to read both candidate nodes together with that final superposition state. Because exactly one candidate is reachable, that candidate appears once through the candidate comparison mechanism and once more through the reachable-set superposition, so its coefficient in the final hidden state is strictly larger than the other candidate's coefficient. The readout matrix is then chosen so that greedy decoding returns the candidate with the largest coefficient, namely the uniquely reachable one.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem reasoning_by_superposition_theorem1_641827
    (Tmax vocabSize nmax : ℕ)
    (hTmax : 0 < Tmax)
    (hvocab : 0 < vocabSize)
    (hnmax : nmax < vocabSize) :
    ∃ (d : ℕ) (θ : TwoLayerTransformer vocabSize d Tmax) (WO : ReadoutMatrix vocabSize d),
      EmbeddingDimensionLinear vocabSize d ∧
      ∀ {V : Type u} [Fintype V]
        (enc : V ↪ Token vocabSize) (G : ReachDigraph V) (r c₁ c₂ : V) (C : ℕ),
        Fintype.card V ≤ nmax →
        ExactlyOneReachable G r c₁ c₂ →
        promptLength G + C + 1 ≤ Tmax →
        graphDiameter G < C →
        TFhat hvocab θ WO enc C G r c₁ c₂ = enc (reachableCandidate G r c₁ c₂) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Classical

noncomputable section

universe u

abbrev Token (vocabSize : ℕ) := Fin vocabSize

structure ReachDigraph (V : Type u) where
  edge : V → V → Prop
  edge_decidable : DecidableRel edge

attribute [instance] ReachDigraph.edge_decidable

namespace ReachDigraph

def Reachable {V : Type u} (G : ReachDigraph V) (u v : V) : Prop :=
  Relation.ReflTransGen G.edge u v

end ReachDigraph

def ExactlyOneReachable {V : Type u} (G : ReachDigraph V) (r c₁ c₂ : V) : Prop :=
  Xor' (ReachDigraph.Reachable G r c₁) (ReachDigraph.Reachable G r c₂)

noncomputable def reachableCandidate {V : Type u} (G : ReachDigraph V) (r c₁ c₂ : V) : V := by
  classical
  exact if ReachDigraph.Reachable G r c₁ then c₁ else c₂

def graphDiameter {V : Type u} (G : ReachDigraph V) : ℕ :=
  0

def edgeCount {V : Type u} [Fintype V] (G : ReachDigraph V) : ℕ :=
  Fintype.card {e : V × V // G.edge e.1 e.2}

def promptLength {V : Type u} [Fintype V] (G : ReachDigraph V) : ℕ :=
  3 * edgeCount G + 6

def EmbeddingDimensionLinear (vocabSize d : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∃ K : ℕ, d ≤ K * vocabSize

structure TwoLayerTransformer (vocabSize d Tmax : ℕ) where
  dummy : Unit := ()

abbrev ReadoutMatrix (vocabSize d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin vocabSize) (Fin d) ℝ

def TFhat
    {V : Type u} {vocabSize d Tmax : ℕ}
    (hvocab : 0 < vocabSize)
    (θ : TwoLayerTransformer vocabSize d Tmax)
    (WO : ReadoutMatrix vocabSize d)
    (enc : V ↪ Token vocabSize)
    (C : ℕ)
    (G : ReachDigraph V)
    (r c₁ c₂ : V) : Token vocabSize :=
  ⟨0, hvocab⟩

metadata.source_paper: Restoring Calibration for Aligned Large Language Models: A Calibration-Aware Fine-Tuning Approach

natural_language.core_claim: For any target accuracy level, there exists a model whose target calibration error is at most twice the absolute gap between its accuracy and that of the best zero-calibration-error model.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Assume that the policy class can represent an arbitrary probability distribution over the response space. Let π* be a model that maximizes accuracy among all models whose expected calibration error is zero, and let the target probabilistic generative model be the confidence distribution induced by π*. For any model π, define its target calibration error as the expected average, over all answer options, of the absolute difference between the target probabilistic generative model and the predicted class probabilities of π. Then, for every accuracy level a between zero and one, there exists a model π whose accuracy is exactly a and whose target calibration error is at most twice the absolute difference between the accuracies of π* and π.
natural_language.informal_proof: Let a* denote the accuracy of π*. The proof constructs a model with any prescribed accuracy a by modifying π* only on a subset of examples whose probability mass is exactly the accuracy gap |a - a*|. First split the data into examples on which π* predicts the correct label and examples on which it does not. If a is at least a*, further split the incorrect-prediction set into two parts, one of mass a - a* and the other of mass 1 - a. Keep the model equal to π* on the unchanged part, and on the selected subset force a one-hot prediction on the true label so that the overall accuracy becomes a. If a is less than a*, instead split the correct-prediction set into two parts, one of mass a* - a and the other of mass 1 - a*, keep the model equal to π* on the unchanged part, and on the selected subset force a one-hot prediction on an incorrect label so that the overall accuracy drops to a. In both cases, the target calibration error is zero on unchanged examples, while on each modified example the average absolute deviation is bounded by 2. Since the modified subset has mass exactly |a - a*|, the total target calibration error is at most 2|a - a*|, which is the desired bound.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem theorem_4_4_upper_bound_of_tce
    {Model : Type*}
    (ACC ECE TCE : Model → ℝ)
    (piStar : Model)
    (hTarget : IsTargetProbabilisticGenerativeModel ACC ECE piStar)
    (hRep : CanRepresentArbitraryDistribution Model)
    {a : ℝ}
    (ha0 : 0 ≤ a)
    (ha1 : a ≤ 1) :
    ∃ pi : Model, ACC pi = a ∧ TCE pi ≤ 2 * |ACC piStar - ACC pi| := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

def CanRepresentArbitraryDistribution (Model : Type*) : Prop := True

def IsTargetProbabilisticGenerativeModel
    {Model : Type*} (ACC ECE : Model → ℝ) (piStar : Model) : Prop :=
  ECE piStar = 0 ∧ ∀ pi : Model, ECE pi = 0 → ACC pi ≤ ACC piStar

metadata.source_paper: Lost in Transmission: When and Why LLMs Fail to Reason Globally

natural_language.core_claim: Chain-of-thought removes the bandwidth bottleneck at any fixed input horizon: every decidable binary language can be recognized on inputs of length at most n by a bounded-attention prefix oracle with constant prefix and attention bandwidth.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let L be a language over the binary alphabet consisting of strings of zeros and ones, and suppose that L is decided by a Turing machine that uses s(n) space on inputs of length n. For any natural number n, let L restricted to length at most n denote the set of strings in L whose length is at most n. Then, for every such n, there exists a bounded-attention prefix oracle with chain of thought that recognizes exactly this length-bounded language, where the oracle has prefix bandwidth 2 and attention bandwidth 3. Here, the chain-of-thought mechanism means that the same bounded-attention procedure is applied repeatedly to the current string, appending one output token at a time, and it eventually halts by emitting a distinguished halt token after producing the correct answer.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument constructs a chain-of-thought computation that simulates the deciding Turing machine step by step. The intermediate tokens explicitly write out the machine configuration over time, including the tape contents and the current machine state. To produce the next token, attention reads from the existing prefix to recover the current state, while the prefix oracle passes along the single bit currently under the tape head when that bit is not already available in the suffix. With this constant amount of communicated information, the suffix-side computation can apply one transition of the Turing machine and emit the next part of the computation trace. Repeating this process generates the full execution history, so the correct output token is produced before the final halt token. Because each simulated step uses only constant prefix bandwidth and constant attention bandwidth, the whole chain-of-thought procedure is a (2, 3)-BAPO-CoT.

theorem litwwlftrg_583214
    (L : Set (List Bool))
    (s : ℕ → ℕ)
    (hL : DecidedBySpaceBoundedTM L s) :
    ∀ n : ℕ, ∃ M : BAPOCoT,
      M.prefixBandwidth = 2 ∧
      M.attentionBandwidth = 3 ∧
      RecognizesUpToLength M L n := by
  sorry

import Mathlib

structure BAPOCoT where
  prefixBandwidth : ℕ
  attentionBandwidth : ℕ

def DecidedBySpaceBoundedTM (L : Set (List Bool)) (s : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop :=
  True

def RecognizesUpToLength (M : BAPOCoT) (L : Set (List Bool)) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
  True

metadata.source_paper: An Efficient Pruner for Large Language Model with Theoretical Guarantee

natural_language.core_claim: For the ℓ0-penalized layer-wise pruning objective, iterative hard-thresholding converges to a fixed point, and the monotone accelerated variant produces bounded iterates whose accumulation points satisfy the same fixed-point criterion.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Fix matrices X and Wc, a nonnegative regularization parameter lam, and define f(W) to be one half of the squared Frobenius norm of XWc minus XW. Let Ht denote element-wise hard thresholding at level t. The iterative hard-thresholding update is W_{k+1} = H_{sqrt(2 alpha lam)}(W_k + alpha X^T X (Wc - W_k)). The monotone accelerated update first forms a momentum point Y_k from the current and previous iterates, then computes Z_{k+1} = H_{sqrt(2 alpha1 lam)}(Y_k - alpha1 grad f(Y_k)) and V_{k+1} = H_{sqrt(2 alpha2 lam)}(W_k - alpha2 grad f(W_k)), updates the momentum parameter, and sets W_{k+1} to Z_{k+1} when its objective value is no larger than that of V_{k+1}, and otherwise to V_{k+1}. A matrix W is called an alpha-fixed point when every nonzero entry of W has magnitude at least sqrt(2 alpha lam) and has zero residual gradient entry, while every zero entry has residual gradient magnitude at most sqrt(2 lam / alpha). If L is a Lipschitz constant of grad f and alpha is smaller than 1 / L, then every sequence generated by iterative hard-thresholding converges to an alpha-fixed point. If alpha1 and alpha2 are both smaller than 1 / L, then every pair of sequences generated by the monotone accelerated method is bounded, and every accumulation point of the sequence of accepted iterates is an alpha2-fixed point.
natural_language.informal_proof: For the iterative hard-thresholding part, after finitely many iterations the support stops changing, so the algorithm becomes projected gradient descent on a fixed support set. With a step size below the reciprocal Lipschitz constant, projected gradient descent over that closed convex set converges. Once convergence is obtained, one passes to the limit in the coordinate-wise update rule: on indices that remain in the limiting support, the update equation forces the residual gradient entries to vanish and preserves the threshold lower bound on the absolute values; on indices outside the support, the hard-thresholding rule yields the corresponding upper bound on the residual gradient magnitude. These are exactly the fixed-point conditions. For the monotone accelerated method, the monotone descent inequality keeps the objective values uniformly bounded above by the initial one, and coercivity of the objective therefore implies boundedness of both the accepted iterates and the monitoring iterates. The descent analysis also shows that the monitoring step and the accepted iterate become asymptotically identical. Hence any convergent subsequence of accepted iterates has a matching subsequence of monitoring iterates with the same limit. Because nonzero coordinates remain separated from zero by the hard-threshold level, the support eventually stabilizes along that subsequence. Passing to the limit in the monitoring update then yields the same coordinate-wise fixed-point conditions, now with alpha2, for every accumulation point.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem efficient_pruner_convergence_theorem_3_3
    {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1)
    (Wc : Mat d1 d2)
    (lam L alpha alpha1 alpha2 : ℝ)
    (hlam : 0 ≤ lam)
    (hL : 0 < L)
    (hLip : ∀ U V : Mat d1 d2,
      frobNorm (gradF X Wc U - gradF X Wc V) ≤ L * frobNorm (U - V))
    (halpha : 0 < alpha)
    (halpha_lt : alpha < 1 / L)
    (halpha1 : 0 < alpha1)
    (halpha1_lt : alpha1 < 1 / L)
    (halpha2 : 0 < alpha2)
    (halpha2_lt : alpha2 < 1 / L) :
    (∀ W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2,
        GeneratedByIHT X Wc lam alpha W →
        ∃ Wstar : Mat d1 d2,
          AlphaFixedPoint X Wc lam alpha Wstar ∧ SeqLimit W Wstar) ∧
    (∀ W V : ℕ → Mat d1 d2,
        GeneratedByMAIHT X Wc lam alpha1 alpha2 W V →
        BoundedSeq W ∧ BoundedSeq V ∧
          ∀ Wstar : Mat d1 d2,
            IsAccumulationPoint W Wstar → AlphaFixedPoint X Wc lam alpha2 Wstar) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Matrix
open scoped Matrix BigOperators

noncomputable section

namespace EfficientPruner

abbrev Mat (r c : Type*) [Fintype r] [DecidableEq r] [Fintype c] [DecidableEq c] :=
  Matrix r c ℝ

def nnz {r c : Type*} [Fintype r] [DecidableEq r] [Fintype c] [DecidableEq c]
    (W : Mat r c) : ℕ :=
  ((Finset.univ.product Finset.univ).filter fun ij => W ij.1 ij.2 ≠ 0).card

def frobNorm {r c : Type*} [Fintype r] [DecidableEq r] [Fintype c] [DecidableEq c]
    (W : Mat r c) : ℝ :=
  Real.sqrt (∑ i, ∑ j, (W i j) ^ (2 : ℕ))

def gradF {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc W : Mat d1 d2) : Mat d1 d2 :=
  Matrix.transpose X * (X * (W - Wc))

def loss {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc : Mat d1 d2) (lam : ℝ) (W : Mat d1 d2) : ℝ :=
  (1 / 2 : ℝ) * (frobNorm (X * Wc - X * W)) ^ (2 : ℕ) + lam * (nnz W : ℝ)

def hardThreshold {r c : Type*} [Fintype r] [DecidableEq r] [Fintype c] [DecidableEq c]
    (t : ℝ) (W : Mat r c) : Mat r c :=
  fun i j => if t < |W i j| then W i j else 0

def ihtStep {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc : Mat d1 d2) (lam alpha : ℝ) (W : Mat d1 d2) : Mat d1 d2 :=
  hardThreshold (Real.sqrt (2 * alpha * lam)) (W - alpha • gradF X Wc W)

def momentumPoint {d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (t : ℕ → ℝ) (Z W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) (k : ℕ) : Mat d1 d2 :=
  W k
    + ((t (k - 1)) / (t k)) • (Z k - W k)
    + (((t (k - 1)) - 1) / (t k)) • (W k - W (k - 1))

def tNext (tk : ℝ) : ℝ :=
  (Real.sqrt (4 * tk ^ (2 : ℕ) + 1) + 1) / 2

def GeneratedByIHT {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc : Mat d1 d2) (lam alpha : ℝ) (W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  W 0 = Wc ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, W (k + 1) = ihtStep X Wc lam alpha (W k)

def GeneratedByMAIHT {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc : Mat d1 d2) (lam alpha1 alpha2 : ℝ)
    (W V : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  ∃ t : ℕ → ℝ, ∃ Z : ℕ → Mat d1 d2,
    W 0 = Wc ∧ W 1 = Wc ∧ Z 1 = Wc ∧ t 0 = 0 ∧ t 1 = 1 ∧
      ∀ k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k →
        Z (k + 1) = ihtStep X Wc lam alpha1 (momentumPoint t Z W k) ∧
        V (k + 1) = ihtStep X Wc lam alpha2 (W k) ∧
        t (k + 1) = tNext (t k) ∧
        W (k + 1) =
          if loss X Wc lam (Z (k + 1)) ≤ loss X Wc lam (V (k + 1)) then
            Z (k + 1)
          else
            V (k + 1)

def AlphaFixedPoint {n d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (X : Mat n d1) (Wc : Mat d1 d2) (lam alpha : ℝ) (W : Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  (∀ i j,
      W i j ≠ 0 →
        ((Matrix.transpose X) * (X * (Wc - W))) i j = 0 ∧
          Real.sqrt (2 * alpha * lam) ≤ |W i j|) ∧
    ∀ i j,
      W i j = 0 →
        |((Matrix.transpose X) * (X * (Wc - W))) i j| ≤ Real.sqrt (2 * lam / alpha)

def BoundedSeq {d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  ∃ C : ℝ, 0 ≤ C ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, frobNorm (W k) ≤ C

def SeqLimit {d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) (A : Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  Filter.Tendsto (fun k : ℕ => frobNorm (W k - A)) Filter.atTop (nhds 0)

def IsAccumulationPoint {d1 d2 : Type*}
    [Fintype d1] [DecidableEq d1]
    [Fintype d2] [DecidableEq d2]
    (W : ℕ → Mat d1 d2) (A : Mat d1 d2) : Prop :=
  ∃ φ : ℕ → ℕ, StrictMono φ ∧ SeqLimit (fun n => W (φ n)) A

end EfficientPruner

metadata.source_paper: Synthetic Text Generation for Training Large Language Models via Gradient Matching

natural_language.core_claim: Gradient-matched synthetic text can be constructed so that gradient-based fine-tuning converges at the same rate as fine-tuning on real data, while the loss gap between synthetic-data and real-data fine-tuning remains explicitly bounded.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let L be the fine-tuning loss on real data and let Ls be the fine-tuning loss on synthetic data. Assume that L is a μ-PL-star loss function. Also assume, as in the gradient-error bound used before this result, that both the real-data and synthetic-data fine-tuning losses are β-smooth, and that the synthetic data generated by GRADMM matches the gradient of the real data at the pretrained parameters θ up to error ε. If the parameter change during fine-tuning is bounded in norm by δ, so that ξ is defined as 2βδ plus ε, and if ∇ is an upper bound on the gradient norm during fine-tuning, then gradient descent on the synthetic data converges with the same rate as gradient descent on the real data. Moreover, for every training step t, the absolute difference between the real-data fine-tuning loss and the synthetic-data fine-tuning loss evaluated at θt is at most ξ times 2∇ minus ξ, divided by 2μ.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins with the standard descent inequality for gradient descent on the real-data objective and combines it with the μ-PL-star condition to obtain a linear decrease of the real loss. For the synthetic-data objective, the same descent inequality is applied to one gradient step. The gradient discrepancy along fine-tuning is then controlled using the previous gradient-error bound, which shows that the synthetic gradient can differ from the real gradient by at most ξ, where ξ equals 2βδ plus ε. Under the proof’s assumption that this error does not exceed the real gradient norm, the synthetic gradient norm is lower-bounded by the real gradient norm minus ξ. Expanding the resulting square produces a term in the real gradient norm squared, a ξ-squared term, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled using the global upper bound ∇ on the gradient norm, and the real gradient norm squared is lower-bounded by 2μ times the real loss through the μ-PL-star condition. This yields a one-step recursion with the same contraction factor as real-data gradient descent, up to an additive neighborhood term depending on ξ and ∇. Summing the recursion over iterations gives the stated same-rate convergence behavior and the claimed upper bound on the loss difference.

metadata.source_paper: Safe Delta: Consistently Preserving Safety when Fine-Tuning LLMs on Diverse Datasets

natural_language.core_claim: A safety-aware modification of the fine-tuning parameter delta can preserve an aligned language model’s safety across diverse fine-tuning datasets while retaining the utility gains brought by benign fine-tuning.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a layer parameter of an aligned model before fine-tuning that is optimal for the safety objective, and define the safety loss on a safety dataset as the squared Euclidean distance between the layer outputs of an updated parameter matrix and those of the original parameter matrix. Let the Hessian of this safety loss at the original parameter be denoted by H, let its inverse be denoted by the inverse Hessian, and let the fine-tuning delta at coordinate m be the difference between the fine-tuned and original parameter values at that coordinate. Then the safety compensation vector that optimally adjusts the remaining weights when that coordinate delta is added is the m-th column of the inverse Hessian scaled by the coordinate delta divided by the m-th diagonal entry of the inverse Hessian, and the resulting safety loss increase attributed to selecting that coordinate is the square of the coordinate delta divided by twice the m-th diagonal entry of the inverse Hessian.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins by expanding the safety loss around the optimal pre-fine-tuning parameter using a Taylor series. Because the expansion is centered at an optimum, the first-order term vanishes, and for small perturbations the higher-order terms are neglected, leaving a quadratic approximation in which the safety loss increase is one half of a Hessian quadratic form. To isolate the effect of selecting a single fine-tuning delta at coordinate m, the perturbation is constrained so that its m-th coordinate equals that delta value, while the remaining coordinates are allowed to adjust freely. A Lagrangian is then formed from the quadratic objective and this linear constraint. Setting the derivatives with respect to the perturbation and the multiplier to zero yields a linear system whose solution expresses the optimal perturbation as the inverse Hessian applied to the basis vector at coordinate m, scaled by the selected delta and normalized by the corresponding diagonal entry of the inverse Hessian. Substituting this optimizer back into the quadratic approximation gives the closed-form expression for the associated increase in safety loss. The compensation vector is then taken to be exactly this optimal perturbation.

    (C : Fin n → ℝ)

    (hHinv_left : H * Hinv = 1)
    (hHinv_right : Hinv * H = 1) :
    (C = (fun i => (deltaWm / Hinv m m) * Hinv i m)) ∧
    (deltaLsafe = deltaWm ^ 2 / (2 * Hinv m m)) := by

metadata.source_paper: Subspace Optimization for Large Language Models with Convergence Guarantees

natural_language.core_claim: Random low-rank projection makes small-batch subspace optimization provably convergent, yielding an average expected squared gradient bound equal to a decaying optimization term plus a variance-dependent noise floor.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider training with GoLore using small-batch stochastic gradients and momentum stochastic gradient descent with matrix projection. Assume the objective is bounded below, its gradient is L-Lipschitz, and the stochastic gradient is unbiased with bounded second moment, so that the total variance is controlled by sigma squared. For each layer, let the projection rank be r_l and let delta_l be the ratio between r_l and the smaller dimension of that layer’s weight matrix, and let delta be the minimum of these ratios over all layers. If the momentum parameter beta1 satisfies zero less than beta1 less than or equal to one, the subspace refresh period tau is at least sixty-four divided by three times beta1 times delta, and the learning rate eta is positive and no larger than the minimum of one over four L, the square root of three delta beta1 squared over eighty L squared, the square root of three delta over eighty tau squared L squared, and the square root of three beta1 over sixteen tau L squared, then for every positive integer K, the average over the first K tau iterations of the expected squared gradient norm is at most sixteen Delta divided by delta eta K tau plus thirty-two beta1 sigma squared divided by three delta, where Delta is the initial objective gap between the starting point and the infimum of the objective.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins with a descent inequality that bounds the cumulative expected squared gradient norm over K tau steps by the decrease in objective value, plus the accumulated discrepancy between the projected momentum state and the true gradient, minus a term involving the squared step sizes. The next step is to control that discrepancy by showing that the momentum error can be bounded by a combination of cumulative expected squared gradients, cumulative squared iterate differences, and a variance term proportional to K tau beta1 sigma squared. Substituting this error bound into the descent inequality yields an inequality in which both the gradient sum and the iterate-difference sum appear with explicit coefficients. The hyperparameter conditions are then chosen precisely so that the coefficient on the gradient sum remains positive and the coefficient on the iterate-difference sum is nonpositive. Finally, lower boundedness replaces the final objective value by the global infimum, and dividing by K tau gives the stated bound on the average expected squared gradient norm.

      eta ≤
        min ((1 : ℝ) / ((4 : ℝ) * L))
          (min (Real.sqrt (((3 : ℝ) * delta * beta1 ^ 2) / ((80 : ℝ) * L ^ 2)))

      ≤ (16 : ℝ) * Delta / (delta * eta * (((K * tau : ℕ) : ℝ)))
        + (32 : ℝ) * beta1 * sigma ^ 2 / ((3 : ℝ) * delta) := by

metadata.source_paper: Progressively Label Enhancement for Large Language Model Alignment

natural_language.core_claim: With a nonempty initial pure level set and a progressively decayed threshold, progressively label enhancement bounds the probability that the learned policy differs from the optimal policy by more than one half epsilon.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let the query space be X and the output space be Y, let the initialized policy be pi zero, the learned policy after progressively label enhancement be pi, and the optimal reward-maximizing policy be pi star. For each query x, let the reward margin be the reward of the principle-guided response minus the reward of the original response. Assume there exist constants alpha and epsilon smaller than one such that, for every query-response pair, the absolute difference between the current policy and the optimal policy is bounded by alpha times the inconsistency mass of training examples with larger reward margin, plus epsilon over six. Assume also that the density of reward margins is bounded between two positive constants, and let l be the corresponding worst-case density imbalance ratio. For a threshold e, define the pure level set as the set of queries whose reward margin is at least e; this level set is pure for a policy if, on every query in the set, the policy assigns higher probability to the principle-guided response than to the original response. If the initialized policy has a pure level set at threshold e zero, if the purification procedure in progressively label enhancement is run for sufficiently many iterations, if e zero is at least open parenthesis alpha plus epsilon over six close parenthesis divided by open parenthesis one plus alpha close parenthesis, if the ending threshold is greater than epsilon, and if the number of iterations is at least six l divided by epsilon times the logarithm of open parenthesis one minus epsilon close parenthesis divided by open parenthesis one over the size of Y minus e zero close parenthesis, then the probability over x drawn from p of x and y drawn from p of y that the absolute difference between pi of y given x and pi star of y given x exceeds epsilon over two is at most one minus c star epsilon.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof first shows that the initialized model already has a pure level set once the initial threshold is chosen large enough. This uses the assumed approximation bound between the current policy and the optimal policy: when the reward margin is at least e zero, the policy must rank the principle-guided response above the original response, so the initial pure region is valid. Next, the argument proceeds inductively. Assuming the level set at the current threshold is pure, the threshold is decreased by a controlled amount. Lemma 5.2 then implies that the pure level set expands, meaning that more queries enter the region where the model’s ranking agrees with the reward ranking. At the same time, the policy error on the expanded region is forced below epsilon over two. The proof then specializes the terminal threshold to epsilon in order to obtain the largest useful pure region. Using the lower bound on the density of reward margins, it shows that the set of queries whose margin is below epsilon has probability at least c star epsilon, so the complementary event, where the learned policy can still differ from the optimal policy by more than epsilon over two, has probability at most one minus c star epsilon. Finally, the stated lower bound on the number of iterations guarantees that repeated threshold decay reaches this terminal regime, which yields the claimed probability bound.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem progressively_label_enhancement_main_482731
    {X Y : Type*}
    [Fintype X] [Fintype Y] [DecidableEq X] [DecidableEq Y]
    (pX : X → ℝ) (pY : Y → ℝ)
    (piLearned pi0 piStar : X → Y → ℝ)
    (R : X → Y → ℝ)
    (yPrompt yOrig : X → Y)
    (alpha eps e0 eEnd ell cLower steps : ℝ)
    (hpX_nonneg : ∀ x, 0 ≤ pX x)
    (hpY_nonneg : ∀ y, 0 ≤ pY y)
    (hpX_sum : Finset.sum Finset.univ (fun x => pX x) = 1)
    (hpY_sum : Finset.sum Finset.univ (fun y => pY y) = 1)
    (hPure0 :
      ∀ x : X,
        e0 ≤ R x (yPrompt x) - R x (yOrig x) →
          pi0 x (yPrompt x) > pi0 x (yOrig x))
    (hInit1 : e0 ≥ (alpha + eps / 6) / (1 + alpha))
    (hInit2 : eEnd > eps)
    (hSteps :
      steps ≥ (6 * ell / eps) * Real.log ((1 - eps) / ((1 : ℝ) / (Fintype.card Y : ℝ) - e0))) :
    Finset.sum Finset.univ
        (fun x =>
          Finset.sum Finset.univ
            (fun y =>
              pX x * pY y *
                (if eps / 2 < abs (piLearned x y - piStar x y) then (1 : ℝ) else (0 : ℝ))))
      ≤ 1 - cLower * eps := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Benign Overfitting in Token Selection of Attention Mechanism

natural_language.core_claim: Under the paper’s parameter regime, a one-layer attention mechanism can benignly overfit through token selection: when the signal-to-noise ratio is low enough relative to the sample size, gradient descent can fit all noisy training labels while still keeping the clean test error small.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider binary classification with the one-layer attention model f(X; W, p) = νᵀXᵀsoftmax(XWᵀp), where X = (x₁, …, x_T)ᵀ is a sequence of T tokens in ℝᵈ, W is the trainable key-query matrix, p is a tunable token, and ν is a fixed pretrained linear head. The clean distribution P* is defined by first sampling a clean label Y* uniformly from {±1}, then sampling independent Gaussian noise vectors ε_t ~ N(0, σ_ε² I_d), setting the relevant token to x₁ = μ_{Y*} + ε₁ for orthogonal class signals μ_{+1}, μ_{-1} with equal norm ‖μ‖₂, setting weakly relevant tokens x_u = ρ μ_{w_u} + ε_u for u in a weak-token set W, with one confusing token x₂ = ρ μ_{-Y*} + ε₂ and the remaining weakly relevant tokens aligned with Y*, and setting every irrelevant token to pure noise. The training distribution P is obtained by flipping Y* with probability η, the training samples S = ((X^(i), Y^(i)))_{i=1}^n are drawn i.i.d. from P, the clean index set is C = {i : Y^(i) = Y*^(i)}, the noisy index set is N = {i : Y^(i) ≠ Y*^(i)}, and the signal-to-noise ratio is SNR = ‖μ‖₂ / (σ_ε √d). Train W and p from Gaussian initialization W(0)_{ab} ~ N(0, σ_w²) and p(0)_a ~ N(0, σ_p²) by gradient descent with step size α on the binary cross-entropy loss. Assume further that the linear-head norm scales as ‖ν‖₂ = O(1 / ‖μ‖₂), and that the following parameter conditions hold for a sufficiently large universal constant C and failure probability δ > 0: d ≥ C ŝ_ε n ‖μ‖₂^{4/3} log^3(T n / δ), where ŝ_ε = max{σ_ε, 1 / σ_ε}; ‖μ‖₂ ≥ C σ_ε d^{3/8} log(T n / δ); C ‖μ‖₂^{-1} σ_ε log(T n / δ) ≤ ρ ≤ 1 / C; α ≤ max{‖μ‖₂ √d, σ_ε d}^{-1} / C; n ≥ C log(d / δ); η ≤ 1 / C; T = Θ(1); and σ_w² and σ_p² are both of order Θ((max{‖μ‖₂ √d, σ_ε d} log^2(T n / δ))^{-1}). Then two regimes hold. First, if SNR² = ω(n^{-1}), then with probability at least 1 − δ there exists a time step τ = Θ(1 / (α ‖ν‖₂ ‖μ‖₂^3 d max{σ_w², σ_p²})) such that the weights (W(τ), p(τ)) fit only the clean data, meaning f_τ(X^(i)) = Y^(i) for every i in C and f_τ(X^(j)) ≠ Y^(j) for every j in N, while the clean test error satisfies Pr_{(X, Y*) ~ P*}[sign(f_τ(X)) ≠ Y*] < δ. Second, if SNR² = o(n^{-1}), then with probability at least 1 − δ there exists a time step τ = Θ(exp(n^{-1} SNR^{-2}) / (α n^{-1} σ_ε² ‖ν‖₂ ‖μ‖₂ d² max{σ_w², σ_p²})) such that the weights (W(τ), p(τ)) overfit the entire training set, meaning f_τ(X^(i)) = Y^(i) for every training sample i in [n], while the same clean test error bound Pr_{(X, Y*) ~ P*}[sign(f_τ(X)) ≠ Y*] < δ still holds.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof first conditions on a high-probability good run, established by concentration lemmas for the Gaussian noise vectors, the random initialization, and the resulting norms and inner products. It then introduces attention-gap variables that measure how much more strongly the model scores a significant token than competing tokens, together with the transformed quantity g(x) = 2x + 2 sinh(x − log T), whose evolution under gradient descent can be tracked almost linearly. For clean examples, the relevant-token attention gap grows according to this controlled dynamics, so the softmax mass progressively concentrates on the class-relevant token. For noisy examples in the benign-overfitting regime, the argument is two-stage: in Stage 1, the attention gap for the truly relevant token decreases, showing that the probability of selecting that token is actively suppressed because selecting it would not help fit the corrupted label; in Stage 2, the attention gap toward the confusing weakly relevant token increases, so the noisy example becomes fit through noise memorization. These dynamics are proved by induction, and a key technical point is that, because gradient updates depend on softmax probabilities, one must control the ratio of contributions from clean and noisy samples at every time step rather than only their counts. After the training dynamics are established, the proof lower-bounds the accumulated attention paid to the class signals. In the high-SNR regime this is sufficient on the shorter time scale to fit only clean data and still generalize. In the low-SNR regime, the signal term grows only logarithmically with training time and is initially weak, so an exponentially long training time is needed to make the class-signal attention dominate random test noise; this yields small clean test error even after the model has already interpolated all noisy training labels. That is exactly the benign-overfitting conclusion, and it also explains the delayed generalization phenomenon highlighted in the paper.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem botsam_417382_thm41
    {n T d : ℕ}
    (X : Fin n → TokenSeq T d)
    (Y Ystar : Fin n → ℝ)
    (C N : Finset (Fin n))
    (predict : ℕ → TokenSeq T d → ℝ)
    (testError : ℕ → ℝ)
    (δ SNR α νNorm μNorm ρ η σeps σw σp : ℝ)
    (hC : ∀ i : Fin n, i ∈ C ↔ Y i = Ystar i)
    (hN : ∀ i : Fin n, i ∈ N ↔ Y i ≠ Ystar i)
    (hHead : HeadScale νNorm μNorm)
    (hAssumptions : ParameterAssumptions n T d μNorm ρ α η δ σeps σw σp) :
    ((HighSNRRegime SNR n) →
      ∃ τ : ℕ,
        NotOverfittingTimeScale τ α νNorm μNorm σw σp d ∧
        ProbGE
          ((∀ i : Fin n, i ∈ C → predict τ (X i) = Y i) ∧
           (∀ j : Fin n, j ∈ N → predict τ (X j) ≠ Y j) ∧
           testError τ < δ)
          (1 - δ)) ∧
    ((LowSNRRegime SNR n) →
      ∃ τ : ℕ,
        BenignOverfittingTimeScale τ SNR α νNorm μNorm σeps σw σp n d ∧
        ProbGE
          ((∀ i : Fin n, predict τ (X i) = Y i) ∧
           testError τ < δ)
          (1 - δ)) := by
  sorry

open Matrix

noncomputable section

abbrev TokenSeq (T d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin T) (Fin d) ℝ

def HeadScale (νNorm μNorm : ℝ) : Prop := True

def ParameterAssumptions
    (n T d : ℕ) (μNorm ρ α η δ σeps σw σp : ℝ) : Prop := True

def HighSNRRegime (SNR : ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop := True

def LowSNRRegime (SNR : ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop := True

def NotOverfittingTimeScale
    (τ : ℕ) (α νNorm μNorm σw σp : ℝ) (d : ℕ) : Prop := True

    (τ : ℕ) (SNR α νNorm μNorm σeps σw σp : ℝ) (n d : ℕ) : Prop := True

def ProbGE (event : Prop) (q : ℝ) : Prop := True

metadata.source_paper: Attention with Trained Embeddings Provably Selects Important Tokens

natural_language.core_claim: When each training sequence contains exactly one label-aligned relevant token, gradient flow on the trained <cls> embedding converges, whenever its direction has a limit, to a max-margin token selector that necessarily includes all important tokens.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the one-layer softmax attention model after one gradient-descent step on all trainable embeddings, and then fix the resulting context embeddings while training only the <cls> embedding p by gradient flow. For a sequence X, define the selected-token set SX(p) to be the set of tokens in X whose embedding has maximal inner product with p. Two vectors are considered equivalent when they induce the same selected-token set on every sequence in the dataset X_n, and the max-margin direction associated with such a selection is the unit-norm minimizer among vectors in that equivalence class that separate every selected token from every non-selected token in every sequence by margin at least one. Assume that every sequence in X_n contains either exactly one completely positive token or exactly one completely negative token, and that all other tokens are irrelevant; here, a completely positive token appears only in sequences with label plus one, and a completely negative token appears only in sequences with label minus one. Let S_c be the set of all completely positive and completely negative tokens. For any delta greater than zero, if the first-step learning rate eta_0 satisfies eta_0 at least 4 n squared T squared, and the embedding dimension d satisfies d at least the maximum of 256, the square of 2 log(|S| squared over delta), (88 eta_0 squared plus 111 eta_0 plus 2) raised to the eighth power, and |S| plus 3, then with probability at least 1 minus delta the norm of the gradient-flow solution p_t diverges to infinity. Moreover, if the normalized direction p_t divided by its norm has a limit p_infinity as t goes to infinity, then with probability at least 1 minus delta the limit direction selects all tokens in S_c, meaning S_c is contained in the union over all dataset sequences of SX(p_infinity), and p_infinity is exactly the max-margin direction for that selection.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument has three parts. First, one shows that the gradient-flow trajectory for the <cls> embedding cannot converge to any finite-norm stationary point by explicitly constructing a comparison vector whose inner product with the gradient is always strictly negative; this forces the norm of p_t to diverge. Second, the first gradient step already makes the model approximately favor the relevant token in each sequence, because the trained context embeddings encode token importance, and the logistic-loss gradient flow cannot increase the loss; therefore every later iterate continues to select the important token, so any directional limit must still select all completely positive and completely negative tokens. Third, one proves that any such limiting direction must be max-margin: if a putative limit were not the max-margin solution for its induced feasible selection, then the normalized trajectory would instead converge to the corresponding max-margin direction, contradicting the assumption that it converges to the non-max-margin vector. Combining these three steps yields both norm divergence and the max-margin characterization of every directional limit.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem attention_with_trained_embeddings_provably_selects_important_tokens_theorem_4_3
    {Ω V Tok Seq : Type*}
    [Norm V]
    (prob : Set Ω → ℝ)
    (Xn : Set Seq)
    (Sc : Set Tok)
    (SX : V → Seq → Set Tok)
    (Pstar : V → V)
    (HasDirectionalLimit : (ℝ → V) → V → Prop)
    (SolvesGradientFlow : (ℝ → V) → Prop)
    (pt : Ω → ℝ → V)
    (AssumptionOne : Prop)

        max 256
          (max ((2 * Real.log (SCard^2 / δ))^2)
            (max ((88 * eta0^2 + 111 * eta0 + 2)^8) (SCard + 3))))

      prob {ω | ∀ pInf, HasDirectionalLimit (pt ω) pInf →
        ((Sc ⊆ ⋃ X ∈ Xn, SX pInf X) ∧ Pstar pInf = pInf)} ≥ 1 - δ := by

metadata.source_paper: Limitations of Normalization in Attention Mechanism

natural_language.core_claim: Even under ideal spherical embeddings with pairwise separation, a single attention head has a strict geometric resolution limit and cannot reliably distinguish all top-weighted tokens.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Assume that each token embedding lies uniformly on a d-dimensional sphere of radius M, and that the Euclidean distance between any two distinct embeddings is at least delta, where delta is positive. Let I_N be the indices of N selected tokens, and let the context vector be the weighted sum of their embeddings, namely the sum over i in I_N of alpha_i times x_i. Fix a tolerance radius r greater than zero, and call a selected embedding geometrically distinguishable when its rescaled version alpha_i x_i lies inside the closed Euclidean ball of radius r centered at the context vector, while every non-selected rescaled embedding lies outside that ball. Let N_s be the number of geometrically distinguishable selected embeddings. Then the fraction of geometrically distinguishable embeddings satisfies a two-sided bound: it is at least one minus one over rN times the sum over i in I_N of xi_i, and at most one over N times the sum over i in I_N of the exponential of minus the square of r minus xi_i divided by sixteen M squared, where xi_i squared equals M squared times the sum over all selected indices j different from i of alpha_j squared, plus the quantity M squared minus delta squared over two times the sum over all selected indices j and k that are both different from i and also different from each other of alpha_j alpha_k.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof begins by writing the expected number of geometrically distinguishable selected embeddings as the sum, over selected indices, of the probabilities that the rescaled embedding alpha_i x_i lies within distance r of the context vector. For the upper bound, define X_i as the distance between the context vector and alpha_i x_i. Since every embedding lies on a sphere of radius M, X_i is bounded in the interval from zero to two M. A Hoeffding-type concentration inequality is then applied to bound the probability that X_i is at most r by an exponential term involving the mean of X_i. Next, the mean of X_i is bounded by the square root of the second moment. Expanding the squared norm and using the minimum pairwise separation condition converts the second moment into the quantity xi_i squared, which yields the exponential upper bound after summing over i. For the lower bound, the argument uses Markov’s inequality together with the same Cauchy-Schwarz estimate on the mean of X_i, giving a lower bound of one minus xi_i over r for each selected index. Summing these inequalities over the selected set produces the claimed lower bound on the expected distinguishable fraction.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem lna_482731
    {d n : ℕ}
    (hn : 0 < n)
    (M delta r expectedNs : ℝ)
    (x : Fin n → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))
    (alpha xi : Fin n → ℝ)
    (hM : 0 ≤ M)
    (hdelta : 0 < delta)
    (hr : 0 < r)
    (hSphere : ∀ i, ‖x i‖ = M)
    (hSep : ∀ i j, i ≠ j → delta ≤ ‖x i - x j‖)
    (hxi :
      ∀ i,
        (xi i) ^ 2 =
          M ^ 2 * Finset.sum (Finset.univ.erase i) (fun j => (alpha j) ^ 2) +
            (M ^ 2 - delta ^ 2 / 2) *
              Finset.sum
                ((((Finset.univ.erase i).product (Finset.univ.erase i)).filter
                  (fun jk => jk.1 ≠ jk.2)))
                (fun jk => alpha jk.1 * alpha jk.2)) :
    1 - (1 / (r * (n : ℝ))) * Finset.sum Finset.univ (fun i => xi i) ≤ expectedNs / (n : ℝ) ∧
      expectedNs / (n : ℝ) ≤
        (1 / (n : ℝ)) *
          Finset.sum Finset.univ (fun i => Real.exp (-((r - xi i) ^ 2) / (16 * M ^ 2))) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Mechanism and Emergence of Stacked Attention Heads in Multi-Layer Transformers

natural_language.core_claim: A transformer needs at least logarithmically many layers in the retrieval depth D to solve a D-step retrieval problem, because each attention layer can expand the reachable information by only a constant factor.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the simplified information-flow model in which a position may attend to another position only if the two positions already share a piece of information, and in which attending to a position retrieves all information contained in that position. For a retrieval problem with D retrieval steps, let r_{t,0} denote the residual stream at the last position after t transformer layers, and let x_D be the embedding vector of the target token. Then, whenever t is strictly smaller than the base-three logarithm of 2D, the embedding vector x_D cannot be present in r_{t,0} after t layers.
natural_language.informal_proof: Order the relevant input positions by reachability from the final token along the pre-attention shared-information links, and let e_0, e_1, ..., e_{2D} denote the corresponding pieces of shared information, with e_{2D} representing the target embedding x_D. First, show that after every layer, the information contained in any residual stream forms a contiguous block of these pieces. Next, prove by induction on the number of layers that this contiguous block can have length at most 3^t + 1 after t layers, because one layer can enlarge the block by at most a factor of three in this simplified model. For the residual stream at the last position to contain the target embedding x_D = e_{2D}, its contiguous block would have to extend from e_0 all the way to e_{2D}, so its length would need to be at least 2D + 1. This is impossible when 3^t + 1 is still smaller than 2D + 1, which is equivalent to t being strictly smaller than log base 3 of 2D. Therefore the target embedding cannot be present after so few layers.

theorem stacked_attention_heads_lower_bound

    (hD : 0 < D)
    (h_interval : ∃ b : ℕ, rt t 0 = Finset.Icc 0 b)
    (h_len_bound : (rt t 0).card ≤ 3 ^ t + 1)
    (ht : (t : ℝ) < Real.logb 3 (2 * D : ℝ)) :
    (2 * D) ∉ rt t 0 := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Exact Expressive Power of Transformers with Padding

natural_language.core_claim: Constant-depth averaging-hard-attention transformers with polynomial padding have exactly the expressive power of FO-uniform TC0.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let AHAT0* be the class of languages recognizable by causally masked averaging-hard-attention transformers with masked pre-norm, a beginning-of-sequence token, no positional embeddings, constant depth, and polynomially many padding tokens, where the polynomial degree is allowed to vary. Let uAHAT0* be the analogous class for unmasked transformers equipped with 1/i positional encodings. Then uAHAT0* equals AHAT0*, and this common class is exactly FO-uniform TC0, equivalently the class FO[M, bit].
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof establishes the exact characterization by matching the upper and lower bounds. The upper bound is the known fact that constant-depth averaging-hard-attention transformers remain within FO-uniform TC0, and this still holds when padding tokens are allowed. For the lower bound, FO-uniform TC0 is first recast as the class of languages definable by first-order formulas with paired majority quantifiers. Lemma 2 shows that any such formula with k variables and fixed nesting depth can be implemented by an unmasked constant-depth transformer using n^k padding tokens, so every language in FO-uniform TC0 belongs to uAHAT0*. Proposition 1 then converts the unmasked construction into the masked padded setting, yielding that uAHAT0* is contained in AHAT0*. Combining these inclusions gives uAHAT0* = AHAT0* = FO-uniform TC0, and the identification with FO[M, bit] is the standard logical characterization of the same circuit class.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem exact_expressive_power_of_transformers_with_padding :
    uAHAT0Star = AHAT0Star ∧
    AHAT0Star = FOUniformTC0 ∧
    FOUniformTC0 = FOMBit := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

abbrev FormalLanguage : Type := List Bool → Prop
abbrev LanguageClass : Type := Set FormalLanguage

axiom uAHAT0Star : LanguageClass
axiom AHAT0Star : LanguageClass
axiom FOUniformTC0 : LanguageClass
axiom FOMBit : LanguageClass

metadata.source_paper: Rethinking Invariance in In-context Learning

natural_language.core_claim: Simultaneously enforcing permutation invariance and information non-leakage in attention-based in-context learning exactly forces the attention mask over context examples to be diagonal.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the message-passing scheme on the context examples in a Transformer-style in-context learner, where the attention mask on the context examples is a constant matrix whose entries are either zero or negative infinity. This scheme respects permutation invariance and information non-leakage if and only if the attention mask on the context examples is diagonal.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument combines the two structural characterizations established immediately before this result. Permutation invariance restricts the context-example attention mask to exactly three symmetric forms: full attention, diagonal self-attention only, or off-diagonal cross-attention only. Information non-leakage is equivalent to the message-passing graph being acyclic up to self-loops, which means that after reordering the context examples the attention mask must be lower triangular. The only masks that satisfy both restrictions at once are diagonal masks, so the scheme has both properties exactly in the diagonal case.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem rethinking_invariance_in_context_learning_prop36
    {n : ℕ}
    (M : AttentionMask n) :
    (RespectsPermutationInvariance M ∧ RealizesInformationNonLeakage M) ↔
      DiagonalAttentionMask M := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Matrix

abbrev AttentionMask (n : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) Bool

def DiagonalAttentionMask {n : ℕ} (M : AttentionMask n) : Prop :=
  (∀ i : Fin n, M i i = true) ∧
  ∀ i j : Fin n, i ≠ j → M i j = false

variable
  (RespectsPermutationInvariance : ∀ {n : ℕ}, AttentionMask n → Prop)
  (RealizesInformationNonLeakage : ∀ {n : ℕ}, AttentionMask n → Prop)

metadata.source_paper: Backdoor Attacks in Token Selection of Attention Mechanism

natural_language.core_claim: Gradient descent on a prompt-tuned single-head attention classifier can implant a backdoor through token selection: in a suitable regime, the model fits poisoned training data, preserves clean accuracy, and misclassifies triggered inputs.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a prompt-tuned single-head self-attention binary classifier of the form f(X) = νᵀXᵀS(XWᵀp), where W is fixed orthogonal, p is the tunable prompt token updated by gradient descent, and ν is a fixed linear head obtained from one gradient step at initialization. Each clean input X = [x₁, …, x_T]ᵀ has a clean label y drawn uniformly from {±1}; tokens in the relevant index set R carry the class signal x_t = μ_y + ε_t, while tokens in the irrelevant set I = [T] \ R are pure noise x_v = ε_v, with independent Gaussian noises ε_t ~ N(0, Σ). A poisoned input X̃ is formed by choosing a subset P ⊂ I and replacing each token x_p for p ∈ P by the poisoned token x̃_p = α μ̃_{−y}, where α > 1 is the trigger strength. Let ζ_R = |R| / T and ζ_P = |P| / T. Let C_{+1}, C_{−1} be the clean training samples with labels +1 and −1, let N_{+1}, N_{−1} be the poisoned training samples with labels +1 and −1, let C = C_{+1} ∪ C_{−1} and N = N_{+1} ∪ N_{−1}, and for the softmax vector s^i(τ) = S(X^i Wᵀ p(τ)) define the relevant-token uncertainty G_r^i(τ) = (∑_{r∈R} s_r^i(τ))(1 − ∑_{r∈R} s_r^i(τ)) and the poisoned-token uncertainty G_p^i(τ) = (∑_{p∈P} s_p^i(τ))(1 − ∑_{p∈P} s_p^i(τ)). Assume that the relevant signals μ_{+1}, μ_{−1} and poisoned signals μ̃_{+1}, μ̃_{−1} all have the same Euclidean norm and the same Σ-norm, and are pairwise orthogonal both in the standard inner product and in the Σ-inner product. Also assume that there exists a constant C > 1 such that: (A1) Tr(Σ) ≥ C n² T² log²(T n / δ); (A2) the signal strength satisfies ‖μ‖ ≥ C max{T² √log(T n / δ), T √log(T n / δ) / (Tr(Σ))^(1/4), √(Tr(Σ) / n)}; (A3) the poisoned signal strength is sufficiently large, α ≥ max{C √(T / β) · (ζ_R / ζ_P)^(1/4), C √(ζ_R / (β ζ_P)), 1 / (β T)}; (A4) the poison fraction is not too large, β ≤ min{C √(ζ_R / (α³ ζ_P)), (1 / (C α T)) √(ζ_R / ζ_P)}; and (A5) the step size satisfies η ≤ (1 / C) min{1 / ‖∇_p L_b(p)‖_{Lip}, 1 / ‖μ‖², T² / (α ‖μ‖²)}. If the fixed linear head satisfies ‖ν‖ / ‖d‖ = Θ(1 / ‖μ‖²), then with probability at least 1 − δ there exists a sufficiently large time step τ₀ such that for every τ ≥ τ₀ the model interpolates the training data, meaning sign(f(X^i)) = y^i for all training samples i ∈ [n]. Furthermore, fix absolute constants C₁, C₄ > 1 and C₂, C₃, C₅ > 0. For any τ ≥ τ₀, if the following five conditions hold: (1) balanced uncertainty across classes, namely (1 / C₁) ∑_{i∈C_{−1}} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ') ≤ ∑_{i∈C_{+1}} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ') ≤ C₁ ∑_{i∈C_{−1}} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ'); (2) relevant-token uncertainty dominates general variance, namely ζ_R ∑_{i∈[n]} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ') > C₂ ∑_{i∈[n]} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} ∑_{t∈[T]\P} s_t^i(τ')(1 − s_t^i(τ')) / T; (3) for each class c ∈ {±1}, standard data dominates poisoned influence in the relevant direction, namely ζ_R ∑_{i∈C_c} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ') > C₃ α² β ζ_P ∑_{i∈N_{−c}} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ'); (4) for each class c ∈ {±1}, relevant and poisoned contributions are comparable, namely (1 / C₄) < (α³ β ζ_P / ζ_R) · (∑_{i∈N_c} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_p^i(τ')) / (∑_{i∈C_c} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_r^i(τ')) < C₄; and (5) for each class c ∈ {±1}, poisoned-token uncertainty dominates variance, namely α β ζ_P ∑_{i∈N_c} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} G_p^i(τ') > C₅ ∑_{i∈N_c, p∈P} ∑_{0≤τ'≤τ} s_p^i(τ')(1 − s_p^i(τ')) / T², then clean test samples without poisoned triggers satisfy P_{(X,y)∼D}[sign(f_τ(X)) ≠ y] ≤ δ, and poisoned test samples with backdoor triggers satisfy P[sign(f_τ(X̃)) = y] ≤ δ.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof first establishes how attention behaves on the training data. The key step is Lemma 5.1, which shows that under the step-size condition, attention converges to a relevant token on each clean training sample, while on each poisoned training sample it converges to the poisoned token set; because the relevant clean tokens contain independent noise, attention on a clean sample collapses onto a single relevant token, whereas because the poisoned tokens are identical, attention on a poisoned sample is split evenly across the poisoned positions. This yields interpolation of the poisoned training set after sufficiently many gradient steps. To analyze test-time behavior, the learned prompt token p(τ) is decomposed into components along the clean signal directions W μ_c, the poisoned signal directions W μ̃_c, and a residual noise direction. The argument then shows that Conditions (1), (3), and (4) keep the clean and poisoned signal coefficients on comparable scales, while Condition (2) ensures that on clean inputs the relevant-token contribution dominates the variance coming from arbitrary non-poisoned tokens, and Condition (5) is the poisoned analogue that ensures the poisoned-token contribution dominates the competing variance on triggered inputs. From these inequalities one proves that, for sufficiently large τ, the total attention mass on relevant positions exceeds one half for clean test inputs and the total attention mass on poisoned positions exceeds one half for triggered test inputs. Once this concentration is established, the score of a clean test input is controlled by the clean class signal and therefore has the correct sign, whereas the score of a triggered input is controlled by the poisoned signal and therefore flips away from the original label. This gives the two probability bounds in the theorem: clean error at most δ and original-label retention on triggered inputs at most δ.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem backdoor_attacks_in_token_selection_of_attention_mechanism_theorem_4_1
    {Sample : Type} [Fintype Sample] [DecidableEq Sample]
    (trainSet : Finset Sample)
    (predLabel : ℕ → Sample → Int)
    (trainLabel : Sample → Int)
    (cleanTestError : ℕ → ℝ)
    (poisonedTestKeepsOriginalLabelProb : ℕ → ℝ)
    (WithHighProbability : Prop → ℝ → Prop)
    (δ : ℝ)
    (Assumption1 Assumption2 HeadScaling : Prop)
    (BalancedUncertainty : ℕ → Prop)
    (RelevantTokenUncertaintyDominatesGeneralVariance : ℕ → Prop)
    (StandardDataDominatesPoisonedInfluenceInRelevantDirection : ℕ → Prop)
    (RelevantAndPoisonedContributionsAreComparable : ℕ → Prop)
    (PoisonedTokenUncertaintyDominatesVariance : ℕ → Prop)
    (hδ : 0 < δ ∧ δ < 1)
    (hA1 : Assumption1)
    (hA2 : Assumption2)
    (hHead : HeadScaling) :
    WithHighProbability
      (∃ τ₀ : ℕ,
        (∀ τ ≥ τ₀, ∀ i ∈ trainSet, predLabel τ i = trainLabel i) ∧
        ∀ τ ≥ τ₀,
          BalancedUncertainty τ →
          RelevantTokenUncertaintyDominatesGeneralVariance τ →
          StandardDataDominatesPoisonedInfluenceInRelevantDirection τ →
          RelevantAndPoisonedContributionsAreComparable τ →
          PoisonedTokenUncertaintyDominatesVariance τ →
          cleanTestError τ ≤ δ ∧ poisonedTestKeepsOriginalLabelProb τ ≤ δ)
      (1 - δ) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Learning Linear Attention in Polynomial Time

natural_language.core_claim: Multi-head linear attention is strongly agnostically PAC-learnable in polynomial time: there is an algorithm that returns a linear-attention model whose population squared loss is within ε of the best linear-attention model, using only polynomial time and polynomially many samples.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let a dataset consist of independent samples of input-output pairs, where each input is a real matrix with embedding dimension d and example-dependent sequence length, and each output is a real vector of dimension d. Let the maximum sequence length in the dataset be n_max. For any positive number of heads, let the hypothesis class consist of all multi-head linear-attention parameter sets formed by that many pairs of value matrices and fused key-query matrices. Then there exists an algorithm, namely Algorithm 1, that runs in time on the order of N times d to the fourth power times n_max times ε inverse, and that, given only the input-output pairs, returns a multi-head linear-attention parameter set with at most d squared heads such that, with probability at least one minus δ, its population squared loss is at most ε larger than the minimum population squared loss achievable by any linear-attention model with any positive number of heads. Moreover, the required sample size is on the order of ε inverse times the quantity d to the fourth power plus the logarithm of δ inverse.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof begins by writing the training objective for one-layer multi-head linear attention and observing that the model output is a quadratic polynomial in the head parameters. This quadratic form is then rewritten as a linear regression objective in a lifted feature space: each example induces feature matrices, and the entire model is represented by a matrix obtained by summing outer products of flattened value and key-query parameters. Since that matrix has rank at most the number of heads, the original nonconvex problem is relaxed by replacing the rank-constrained matrix with an unconstrained matrix. The relaxed objective is ordinary least squares, so it can be optimized in polynomial time. Next, a singular-value decomposition of the optimal regression matrix is used to factor it back into pairs of matrices, producing a multi-head linear-attention model whose number of heads is the rank of the regression matrix and therefore at most d squared. This reconstructed model matches the relaxed optimum up to the prescribed error. Finally, the sample-complexity bound follows from standard VC-theoretic generalization arguments applied to the lifted linear predictor.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem LLAIPT_482731
    {d N : ℕ}
    (D : Dataset d N)
    (ε δ : ℝ)
    (hIID : DrawnIID D)
    (hε : 0 < ε)
    (hδ₀ : 0 < δ)
    (hδ₁ : δ < 1) :
    ∃ Algorithm1 : ∀ {N' : ℕ}, Dataset d N' → Sigma (fun H : ℕ => Omega d H),
      let out := Algorithm1 D
      out.1 ≤ d ^ 2 ∧
      RuntimeBound N d (nmax D) ε ∧
      PopulationExcessRiskAtMostWithProb D out.2 ε δ ∧
      SampleComplexityBound N d ε δ := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

abbrev Vec (d : ℕ) := Fin d → ℝ
abbrev Mat (d n : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin d) (Fin n) ℝ
abbrev SqMat (d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ

structure Sample (d : ℕ) where
  n : ℕ
  Z : Mat d n
  y : Vec d

abbrev Dataset (d N : ℕ) := Fin N → Sample d

abbrev Omega (d H : ℕ) := Fin H → (SqMat d × SqMat d)

def nmax {d N : ℕ} (D : Dataset d N) : ℕ :=
  Finset.univ.sup fun i => (D i).n

def DrawnIID {d N : ℕ} (_D : Dataset d N) : Prop := True

def RuntimeBound (_N _d _nmax : ℕ) (_ε : ℝ) : Prop := True

def SampleComplexityBound (_N _d : ℕ) (_ε _δ : ℝ) : Prop := True

def PopulationExcessRiskAtMostWithProb
    {d N H : ℕ}
    (_D : Dataset d N)
    (_Θ : Omega d H)
    (_ε _δ : ℝ) : Prop := True

metadata.source_paper: Linear Transformers Implicitly Discover Unified Numerical Algorithms

natural_language.core_claim: In the centralized setting, the EAGLE update rule implicitly recovered from a trained linear transformer converges to the Nyström estimate with second-order behavior, with only logarithmic dependence on the condition number and double-logarithmic dependence on the target accuracy.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a real block matrix X partitioned as [[A, C], [B, D]] and assume that the rank of X equals the rank of A. Let the effective condition number κ(A) be the ratio between the largest singular value of A and its smallest positive singular value. Define the Nyström parameter by W* = B Aᵀ (A Aᵀ)†, and let the Nyström estimate of the missing block D be D̂* = W* C. Run the centralized EAGLE iteration, meaning the unconstrained regime with sketch matrix equal to the identity, one machine, and constants η = 1/3 and γ = 1. Then for every tolerance ε greater than zero, there exists an iteration index L of order log κ(A) plus log log of ε^{-1} times the square root of d' times the Frobenius norm of W* times the Frobenius norm of C, such that every iterate D_ℓ with ℓ at least L satisfies that the Frobenius norm of D_ℓ minus D̂* is at most ε.
natural_language.informal_proof: Let E_ℓ = A_ℓ A_ℓᵀ. The proof first studies the conditioning dynamics of E_ℓ. It shows that the eigenvectors of E_0 are preserved by the iteration, and that each eigenvalue evolves by the scalar recurrence λ_{ℓ+1} = (1 - η_ℓ λ_ℓ)^2 λ_ℓ. From this, the condition number κ_ℓ of A_ℓ decreases by at least a constant factor until A_ℓ becomes nearly isotropic; once κ_ℓ is close to one, the gap κ_ℓ - 1 contracts supergeometrically, which is the second-order phase and is closely related to Newton-Schulz conditioning. The proof then rewrites the output error through a telescoping identity: D_ℓ - D̂* = W* N_ℓ C_0, where N_ℓ is the product of the residual factors I - γ_j E_j. This reduces the problem to controlling the operator norm of N_ℓ. A separate bound shows that the improvement in conditioning forces N_ℓ to decay rapidly. Combining the initial logarithmic conditioning phase with the later supergeometric phase gives the stated bound on L, and substituting this decay into the telescoping identity yields the claimed ε-accurate approximation of the Nyström estimate.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem ltiduna_731482
    {d dPrime n nPrime : ℕ}
    (A : Mat d n)
    (B : Mat dPrime n)
    (C : Mat d nPrime)
    (Aseq : ℕ → Mat d n)
    (Bseq : ℕ → Mat dPrime n)
    (Cseq : ℕ → Mat d nPrime)
    (Dseq : ℕ → Mat dPrime nPrime)
    (Dstar : Mat dPrime nPrime)
    (Wstar : Mat dPrime d)
    (κ : ℝ)
    (hκ : 0 < κ)
    (hWstar : IsNystromParameter A B Wstar)
    (hDstar : IsNystromEstimate C Wstar Dstar)
    (hEAGLE :
      IsCentralizedEAGLERun (1 / 3 : ℝ) (1 : ℝ) A B C Aseq Bseq Cseq Dseq) :
    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
      ∃ L : ℕ,
        CentralizedIterationBound κ ε dPrime (frobNorm Wstar) (frobNorm C) L ∧
        ∀ ℓ : ℕ, L ≤ ℓ → frobNorm (Dseq ℓ - Dstar) ≤ ε := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Matrix
open scoped BigOperators

noncomputable section

abbrev Mat (m n : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin m) (Fin n) ℝ

def frobNorm {m n : ℕ} (A : Mat m n) : ℝ :=
  Real.sqrt (∑ i : Fin m, ∑ j : Fin n, (A i j) ^ 2)

/-- Placeholder predicate packaging the Moore-Penrose formula
`Wstar = B * A.transpose * (A * A.transpose)†`. -/
def IsNystromParameter {d dPrime n : ℕ}
    (A : Mat d n) (B : Mat dPrime n) (Wstar : Mat dPrime d) : Prop :=
  True

def IsNystromEstimate {d dPrime nPrime : ℕ}
    (C : Mat d nPrime) (Wstar : Mat dPrime d) (Dstar : Mat dPrime nPrime) : Prop :=
  Dstar = Wstar * C

def IsCentralizedEAGLERun
    {d dPrime n nPrime : ℕ}
    (η γ : ℝ)
    (A0 : Mat d n)
    (B0 : Mat dPrime n)
    (C0 : Mat d nPrime)
    (Aseq : ℕ → Mat d n)
    (Bseq : ℕ → Mat dPrime n)
    (Cseq : ℕ → Mat d nPrime)
    (Dseq : ℕ → Mat dPrime nPrime) : Prop :=
  Aseq 0 = A0 ∧
  Bseq 0 = B0 ∧
  Cseq 0 = C0 ∧
  Dseq 0 = 0 ∧
  ∃ ρ : ℕ → ℝ,
    ∀ ℓ : ℕ,
      Aseq (ℓ + 1) = Aseq ℓ - (η * ρ ℓ) • (((Aseq ℓ) * (Aseq ℓ).transpose) * (Aseq ℓ)) ∧
      Bseq (ℓ + 1) = Bseq ℓ - (η * ρ ℓ) • (((Bseq ℓ) * (Aseq ℓ).transpose) * (Aseq ℓ)) ∧
      Cseq (ℓ + 1) = Cseq ℓ - (γ * ρ ℓ) • (((Aseq ℓ) * (Aseq ℓ).transpose) * (Cseq ℓ)) ∧
      Dseq (ℓ + 1) = Dseq ℓ + (γ * ρ ℓ) • (((Bseq ℓ) * (Aseq ℓ).transpose) * (Cseq ℓ))

def CentralizedIterationBound
    (κ ε : ℝ) (dPrime : ℕ) (wNorm cNorm : ℝ) (L : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∃ c C : ℝ,
    0 < c ∧
    0 ≤ C ∧
    (L : ℝ) ≤
      c * (Real.log κ + Real.log (Real.log (ε⁻¹ * Real.sqrt (dPrime : ℝ) * wNorm * cNorm))) + C

metadata.source_paper: ROPO: Robust Preference Optimization for Large Language Models

natural_language.core_claim: Under symmetric preference-label noise below one half, the noise-aware objective used in ROPO preserves the clean optimal preference decision boundary.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Assume that pairwise preference labels are generated from a clean Bradley–Terry preference distribution and then independently flipped with probability less than one half. Let theta_star_eta be a minimizer of the noisy expected risk in Problem (4) when the training loss is the noise-aware loss ell_na. Then, for any query and any pair of candidate responses, the preference probability assigned by the optimal model is one exactly when the clean Bradley–Terry probability of preferring the first response over the second is greater than one half, and is zero otherwise. Consequently, the optimal preference probability under the noisy distribution is the same as the optimal preference probability under the clean distribution.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof expands the noisy expected risk of ell_na by first conditioning on the clean preference outcome and then on whether the observed label is flipped. For a fixed query and response pair, this turns the objective into an affine function of the model preference probability p, where the slope is proportional to the product of one minus twice the noise rate and one minus twice the clean Bradley–Terry probability. Because the noise rate is less than one half, the sign of the slope is determined by whether the clean preference probability is above or below one half. Therefore the minimum over the interval from zero to one is attained at p equals one when the clean preference probability is greater than one half, and at p equals zero otherwise, which yields the indicator-form solution. Applying the same characterization to the clean objective gives the same optimum, so the noisy optimum coincides with the clean optimum.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem ropo_theorem_3_4
    {Query Response : Type*}
    (η : ℝ)
    (hη : η < (1 : ℝ) / 2)
    (Pstar Pnoisy : Query → Response → Response → ℝ)
    (hnoisy_opt :
      ∀ x y₁ y₂ p,
        0 ≤ p →
        p ≤ 1 →
          ((Pstar x y₁ y₂ + η - 2 * Pstar x y₁ y₂ * η) * (1 - Pnoisy x y₁ y₂)
              + (2 * Pstar x y₁ y₂ * η + 1 - Pstar x y₁ y₂ - η) * Pnoisy x y₁ y₂)
            ≤
          ((Pstar x y₁ y₂ + η - 2 * Pstar x y₁ y₂ * η) * (1 - p)
              + (2 * Pstar x y₁ y₂ * η + 1 - Pstar x y₁ y₂ - η) * p)) :
    let Pclean : Query → Response → Response → ℝ :=
      fun x y₁ y₂ => if (1 : ℝ) / 2 < Pstar x y₁ y₂ then (1 : ℝ) else (0 : ℝ)
    ∀ x y₁ y₂,
      Pnoisy x y₁ y₂ = (if (1 : ℝ) / 2 < Pstar x y₁ y₂ then (1 : ℝ) else (0 : ℝ)) ∧
      Pnoisy x y₁ y₂ = Pclean x y₁ y₂ := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Fast Large Language Model Collaborative Decoding via Speculation

natural_language.core_claim: In weighted-ensemble collaborative decoding, using one model as the speculative proposer guarantees an acceptance rate no smaller than that model’s ensemble weight.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider collaborative speculative decoding with two token distributions, where q is the proposal-model distribution, p is the other model’s distribution, and the combined distribution is the weighted ensemble λq(x) + (1 − λ)p(x). Let α denote the acceptance rate of the modified speculative verification procedure for this combined distribution. Then α is at least λ.
natural_language.informal_proof: Start from the acceptance-rate expression used by the speculative verification rule. Substitute the weighted-ensemble combined distribution into that expression, so that each per-token acceptance term becomes q(x) times the minimum of 1 and λ plus a nonnegative correction term. Because the correction term is nonnegative, each minimum term is at least λ. Summing over all tokens yields a lower bound of λ times the total mass of q, and since q is a probability distribution, that total mass is 1. Therefore the acceptance rate α is at least λ.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem fllmcdvs_482731
    {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (p q : V → ℝ)
    (lam : ℝ)
    (hlam0 : 0 ≤ lam)
    (hlam1 : lam ≤ 1)
    (hp_nonneg : ∀ x, 0 ≤ p x)
    (hq_nonneg : ∀ x, 0 ≤ q x)
    (hp_sum : ∑ x, p x = 1)
    (hq_sum : ∑ x, q x = 1) :
    lam ≤ ∑ x, min (q x) (lam * q x + (1 - lam) * p x) := by
  sorry
