metadata.source_paper: Softmax is not Enough (for Sharp Size Generalisation)

natural_language.core_claim: For bounded logits and any positive temperature, each individual softmax coefficient must decay on the order of one over the number of items, so softmax attention inevitably becomes dispersed rather than remaining sharp as problem size grows.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let, for each problem size, a collection of n logits be fed into a softmax function with temperature greater than zero, and suppose every logit is uniformly bounded between two fixed real constants. Then, as the number of items tends to infinity, the softmax probability assigned to any particular item is on the order of one over n. Equivalently, all attention coefficients must disperse as problem size increases.
natural_language.informal_proof: Denote the softmax coefficient of item k by alpha. Since every logit lies between the same lower bound and upper bound, the numerator of alpha is at most the exponential of the upper bound divided by the temperature, while the denominator is at least n times the exponential of the lower bound divided by the temperature. This gives an upper bound of a constant times one over n. Symmetrically, the numerator is at least the exponential of the lower bound divided by the temperature, while the denominator is at most n times the exponential of the upper bound divided by the temperature, yielding a lower bound of another constant times one over n. Because both constants are independent of n, the coefficient of every item is Theta of one over n, so dispersion is unavoidable.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem softmax_must_disperse
    (θ m M : ℝ)
    (hθ : 0 < θ)
    (e : ∀ N : ℕ, Fin (N.succ) → ℝ)
    (hbound : ∀ N (k : Fin (N.succ)), m ≤ e N k ∧ e N k ≤ M) :
    ∀ N (k : Fin (N.succ)),
      (1 / (N.succ : ℝ)) * Real.exp ((m - M) / θ) ≤ softmaxCoeff θ (e N) k ∧
      softmaxCoeff θ (e N) k ≤ (1 / (N.succ : ℝ)) * Real.exp ((M - m) / θ) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

open scoped BigOperators

def softmaxCoeff (θ : ℝ) {n : ℕ} (e : Fin n → ℝ) (k : Fin n) : ℝ :=
  Real.exp (e k / θ) / ∑ j : Fin n, Real.exp (e j / θ)

metadata.source_paper: Fundamental Limits of Prompt Tuning Transformers: Universality, Capacity and Efficiency

natural_language.core_claim: Prompt tuning on an extremely simple transformer architecture, with just one head and one self-attention layer, is already a universal approximator for continuous Lipschitz sequence-to-sequence functions.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Fix any norm exponent at least one and any error tolerance greater than zero. Let FC be the class of continuous sequence-to-sequence functions from d-by-L sequences with entries in the unit interval to d-by-L sequences with entries in the unit interval, under the assumption that every function in FC is C-Lipschitz with respect to the entrywise norm. Let T_A^{1,1,4} denote the transformer class consisting of a single-head, single-layer, size-one self-attention module together with on the order of one over epsilon to the power d times (Lp plus L) feed-forward layers, each feed-forward layer having four hidden neurons. Then there exists a transformer in T_A^{1,1,4} such that, for every target function in FC, one can choose a soft prompt of shape d by Lp whose prompted output, after discarding the prompt positions, approximates the target function with error at most epsilon.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument proceeds by a chained reduction through quantization. First, every target sequence-to-sequence function in the C-Lipschitz class is approximated by a quantized piecewise-constant function on a finite grid, with the quantization chosen so that this first approximation contributes at most half of the final error budget. Second, a surrogate quantized function is constructed on prompted inputs so that different soft prompts select different quantized target functions; this turns prompt tuning into a mechanism for indexing the desired approximation. Third, the contextual-mapping property of a single-head, single-layer attention module is used to assign each prompted quantized input a unique context-dependent representation, and a stack of feed-forward layers with four hidden neurons per layer maps these representations to the surrogate outputs with another error contribution of at most half of the budget. Adding the two approximation errors gives the required epsilon bound.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem fundamental_limits_of_prompt_tuning_transformers_theorem_2_3
    {d L Lp : ℕ}
    {C α ε : ℝ}
    (hα : 1 ≤ α)
    (hε : 0 < ε)
    (dα : SeqMap d L → SeqMap d L → ℝ)
    (FC : Set (SeqMap d L))
    (hFC :
      ∀ ⦃f : SeqMap d L⦄,
        f ∈ FC →
          IsCLipschitzSeq2Seq C f ∧ MapsUnitCubeToItself f) :
    ∃ τ : TA1114Transformer d Lp L,
      ∀ ⦃f : SeqMap d L⦄,
        f ∈ FC →
          ∃ P : PromptMatrix d Lp,
            dα (postPrompt τ P) f ≤ ε := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

abbrev SeqMatrix (d L : ℕ) := Fin d → Fin L → ℝ
abbrev PromptMatrix (d Lp : ℕ) := Fin d → Fin Lp → ℝ
abbrev SeqMap (d L : ℕ) := SeqMatrix d L → SeqMatrix d L

def InUnitCube {d L : ℕ} (Z : SeqMatrix d L) : Prop :=
  ∀ i j, 0 ≤ Z i j ∧ Z i j ≤ 1

def MapsUnitCubeToItself {d L : ℕ} (f : SeqMap d L) : Prop :=
  ∀ Z, InUnitCube Z → InUnitCube (f Z)

def IsCLipschitzSeq2Seq {d L : ℕ} (C : ℝ) (f : SeqMap d L) : Prop :=
  LipschitzWith (Real.toNNReal C) f

structure TA1114Transformer (d Lp L : ℕ) where
  run : PromptMatrix d Lp → SeqMatrix d L → SeqMatrix d L

def postPrompt {d Lp L : ℕ}
    (τ : TA1114Transformer d Lp L)
    (P : PromptMatrix d Lp) : SeqMap d L :=
  fun Z => τ.run P Z

metadata.source_paper: RNNs are not Transformers (Yet): The Key Bottleneck on In-Context Retrieval

natural_language.core_claim: Even with arbitrary-length chain-of-thought, RNNs with sublinear memory cannot match constant-size Transformers on fundamental in-context retrieval tasks, showing that in-context retrieval is the key bottleneck.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Fix any one of the following four tasks. Index asks for the type of the i-th token in a length-n sequence when given the query token i. Associative Recall asks, for a length-n sequence of tokens from [n] and a query token q in [n], for the token that immediately follows q in the sequence. c-gram retrieval asks, for a length-n sequence and a queried (c - 1)-gram that appears as the prefix of some c-gram in that sequence, for the last token of that c-gram. Counting asks, for a length-n sequence, a query token q in [n], and a query number t in the natural numbers, whether the number of occurrences of q in the sequence is greater than t. For any such task, there exists a constant-size Transformer that solves it. In contrast, any RNN whose memory is o(n) bits cannot solve the size-n version of the task, regardless of the length of the chain of thought, once n is sufficiently large.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof proceeds case by case. For the lower bound, the recurrent computation is viewed as a streaming process and then as a one-way communication protocol. One party runs the RNN on the context part of the input and sends only the hidden state to the other party, who continues the simulation on the query part and, if needed, through the entire chain of thought until the final answer is produced. For Index, this would yield an o(n)-bit protocol for recovering the queried bit of an n-bit string, contradicting the standard information-theoretic lower bound. Associative Recall, c-gram retrieval, and Counting are then reduced to Index by explicit encodings, so the same impossibility follows for all three tasks. For the upper bound, constant-size Transformers are constructed explicitly for each task: Index is solved by a matching mechanism that aligns the final query with the relevant earlier position; Associative Recall and c-gram retrieval first copy the needed local predecessor information and then apply the same matching idea; Counting uses copying together with a counting block and a final comparison. Therefore, constant-size Transformers solve all four tasks, whereas o(n)-bit-memory RNNs fail on sufficiently large inputs even if arbitrary-length chain-of-thought is allowed.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem RTYKBICR_538214
    (T : RetrievalTask) :
    (∃ tr : TransformerModel,
        ConstantSizeTransformer tr ∧
        ∀ n : ℕ, SolvesTransformerOnSize tr T n) ∧
    (∀ r : RNNModel,
        LittleOMemory r →
        ∃ N : ℕ,
          ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
          ∀ cotLen : ℕ, ¬ SolvesRNNWithCoT r T n cotLen) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

inductive RetrievalTask where
  | index
  | associativeRecall
  | cgramRetrieval
  | counting
  deriving DecidableEq, Repr

structure TransformerModel where
  carrier : Unit := ()

structure RNNModel where
  carrier : Unit := ()

def ConstantSizeTransformer (_ : TransformerModel) : Prop := True

def LittleOMemory (_ : RNNModel) : Prop := True

def SolvesTransformerOnSize (_ : TransformerModel) (_ : RetrievalTask) (_ : ℕ) : Prop := True

def SolvesRNNWithCoT (_ : RNNModel) (_ : RetrievalTask) (_ : ℕ) (_ : ℕ) : Prop := True

metadata.source_paper: Towards Understanding Token Selection in Self-Attention: Successes and Pitfalls in Learning Random Walks

natural_language.core_claim: For deterministic walks on a circle, a one-layer self-attention transformer trained from zero by gradient descent cannot outperform random guessing in next-token prediction, because training keeps its attention uniform over all history tokens.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the deterministic-walk task on a circle with K nodes, where the initial state is drawn uniformly from the K nodes, each later state is obtained by moving exactly one step counterclockwise, the token xi is the one-hot encoding of the i-th state for each history position, the label y is the final state, and the sequence length satisfies N equals r times K plus 1 for some integer r at least 1. Consider the one-layer transformer that forms the data matrix X by appending a zero column to the history tokens, augments it with the positional-embedding matrix to obtain the full input, outputs fθ(X) = V X S(X̃ᵀ W x̃N), predicts the smallest label attaining the maximum score, and is trained from zero initialization by gradient descent on the population loss given by the expectation of ℓ applied to the score of the true label. Then, for every loss function, every learning rate greater than zero, and every training iteration T at least zero, the prediction accuracy is exactly 1 divided by K. Moreover, for every sample generated by the deterministic-walk task, the value matrix at iteration T is proportional to the all-ones K by K matrix, and the softmax attention weights assigned to the first N minus 1 tokens are all equal.
natural_language.informal_proof: At initialization, the softmax assigns equal weight to every history token, so the first update of the value matrix is driven only by the average of all past one-hot tokens. Because N minus 1 equals rK in the deterministic walk, the history visits every node exactly r times, so this average is the same uniform vector for every training sample. Therefore the first updated value matrix has all entries equal, while the attention matrix does not change because the value matrix starts at zero. Repeating the same symmetry argument for the second gradient step shows that the value matrix remains all-equal, and since every history token is equally informative for predicting the target, the update of the attention parameter keeps all attention weights equal as well. An induction then yields that at every training step the value matrix stays proportional to the all-ones matrix and the attention over all history positions stays uniform. As a result, the transformer always averages all history tokens and produces identical prediction scores for all possible labels. Under the prediction rule that breaks ties by choosing the smallest maximizing label, the model is therefore no better than a random guess on a uniformly distributed target, so its accuracy is exactly 1 divided by K.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem tutssplrw_571842
    (K N M r : ℕ)
    (loss : ℝ → ℝ)
    (η : ℝ)
    (trained : ℕ → TransformerState K N M)
    (task2PredictionAccuracy : ℕ → ℝ)
    (generatedByTask2 : Task2Sample K N → Prop)
    (attentionAt : ∀ t : ℕ, Task2Sample K N → Fin (N - 1) → ℝ)
    (hK : 0 < K)
    (hr : 1 ≤ r)
    (hN : N = r * K + 1)
    (hη : 0 < η)
    (htrain : TrainedByZeroInitGradientDescentOnPopulationLoss loss η trained) :
    ((∀ T : ℕ, task2PredictionAccuracy T = (1 : ℝ) / (K : ℝ)) ∧
      (∀ T : ℕ,
        AlmostSureTask2 generatedByTask2 (fun sample =>
          ProportionalTo (trained T).V (allOnesMatrix K K) ∧
          UniformAttention N (attentionAt T sample)))) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

noncomputable section

structure Task2Sample (K N : ℕ) where
  X : Matrix (Fin K) (Fin N) ℝ
  y : Fin K

structure TransformerState (K N M : ℕ) where
  V : Matrix (Fin K) (Fin K) ℝ
  W : Matrix (Fin (K + M)) (Fin (K + M)) ℝ

def allOnesMatrix (m n : ℕ) : Matrix (Fin m) (Fin n) ℝ :=
  fun _ _ => 1

def ProportionalTo {m n : ℕ} (A B : Matrix (Fin m) (Fin n) ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∃ c : ℝ, A = c • B

def UniformAttention (N : ℕ) (a : Fin (N - 1) → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ i j, a i = a j

def TrainedByZeroInitGradientDescentOnPopulationLoss
    {K N M : ℕ}
    (loss : ℝ → ℝ)
    (η : ℝ)
    (trained : ℕ → TransformerState K N M) : Prop :=
  True

def AlmostSureTask2
    {K N : ℕ}
    (generatedByTask2 : Task2Sample K N → Prop)
    (p : Task2Sample K N → Prop) : Prop :=
  ∀ sample, generatedByTask2 sample → p sample

metadata.source_paper: In-Context Learning under Distribution Shift: Optimal Attention Temperature for Transformers

natural_language.core_claim: Under distribution shift, the attention temperature that minimizes the in-context generalization error of a pretrained Transformer with linearized softmax attention is not universal but has a closed-form expression determined by the shifted data statistics and the pretrained parameters.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a pretrained Transformer with linearized softmax attention for in-context linear regression, in a setting where the input and task distributions are well behaved, the context length and input dimension jointly diverge, and the pretrained parameters satisfy the boundedness condition ||M_11|| ≤ c d, m_21 = 0, ||v_21|| ≤ c/(d l), and |v_22| ≤ c d for some constant c > 0. At test time, let the input, task, and noise distributions be N(μ_x, Σ_x), N(μ_w, Σ_w), and N(0, σ^2), respectively. Define A = Σ_x + μ_x μ_x^⊤ and B = Σ_w + μ_w μ_w^⊤. Define \hat B = v_22 μ_w v_21^⊤ + v_22 v_21 μ_w^⊤ + v_22^2 B, F_1 = (Σ_x \hat B + ((v_22^2 σ^2 + Tr(\hat B Σ_x))/l) I) Σ_x, and F_2 = (μ_w v_21^⊤ + v_22 B) Σ_x. Then the inference temperature that minimizes the generalization error is τ_optimal = 2 Tr(A M_11^⊤ F_1 M_11) / Tr(A(F_2 M_11 + M_11^⊤ F_2^⊤)), provided that Tr(A(F_2 M_11 + M_11^⊤ F_2^⊤)) > 0 and Tr(A M_11^⊤ F_1 M_11) > 0.
natural_language.informal_proof: Start from the closed-form generalization error established earlier: G(V, M) = Tr(A M_11^⊤ F_1 M_11)/τ^2 - Tr(A(F_2 M_11 + M_11^⊤ F_2^⊤))/τ + Tr(A B) + σ^2. Only the first two terms depend on the temperature τ. Write a = Tr(A M_11^⊤ F_1 M_11) and b = Tr(A(F_2 M_11 + M_11^⊤ F_2^⊤)), so the τ-dependent part is a/τ^2 - b/τ. Differentiate this expression with respect to τ, set the derivative equal to zero, and solve for the critical point. This gives τ = 2a/b, namely τ_optimal = 2 Tr(A M_11^⊤ F_1 M_11) / Tr(A(F_2 M_11 + M_11^⊤ F_2^⊤)). The positivity assumptions ensure that this temperature is admissible and yields the minimizing choice.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem optimal_attention_temperature_under_distribution_shift
    {n : Type*} [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    (μx μw v21 : n → ℝ)
    (Sx Sw M11 : Matrix n n ℝ)
    (v22 σ l : ℝ) :
    let A : Matrix n n ℝ := Sx + Matrix.vecMulVec μx μx
    let B : Matrix n n ℝ := Sw + Matrix.vecMulVec μw μw
    let Bhat : Matrix n n ℝ :=
      v22 • Matrix.vecMulVec μw v21 +
      v22 • Matrix.vecMulVec v21 μw +
      (v22 ^ (2 : ℕ)) • B
    let F1 : Matrix n n ℝ :=
      (Sx * Bhat +
        ((v22 ^ (2 : ℕ) * σ ^ (2 : ℕ) + Matrix.trace (Bhat * Sx)) / l) •
          (1 : Matrix n n ℝ)) *
        Sx
    let F2 : Matrix n n ℝ :=
      (Matrix.vecMulVec μw v21 + v22 • B) * Sx
    let G : ℝ → ℝ := fun t =>
      Matrix.trace (A * M11.transpose * F1 * M11) / t ^ (2 : ℕ) -
        Matrix.trace (A * (F2 * M11 + M11.transpose * F2.transpose)) / t +
        Matrix.trace (A * B) + σ ^ (2 : ℕ)
    0 < l →
    0 < Matrix.trace (A * M11.transpose * F1 * M11) →
    0 < Matrix.trace (A * (F2 * M11 + M11.transpose * F2.transpose)) →
    ∀ t > 0,
      G
          (2 * Matrix.trace (A * M11.transpose * F1 * M11) /
            Matrix.trace (A * (F2 * M11 + M11.transpose * F2.transpose))) ≤
        G t := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open scoped Matrix

metadata.source_paper: On Zero-Initialized Attention: Optimal Prompt and Gating Factor Estimation

natural_language.core_claim: Under mild regularity assumptions, zero-initialized attention with non-linear prompts admits least-squares estimation of the prompt mixing measure and the gating factor at the parametric order sqrt(log n / n) in probability.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider independent and identically distributed input-output pairs generated by a zero-initialized attention regression model with non-linear prompts, where the noise variables are independent Gaussian with zero conditional mean and isotropic conditional variance, the inputs are independent and identically distributed from an underlying distribution, the regression function is a prefix mixture-of-experts model consisting of a fixed collection of pre-trained experts together with an unknown prompt-dependent component, and each unknown prompt is first transformed element-wise by an activation function before entering the pre-trained projection maps. Let the unknown prompt component be represented by a true finite mixing measure together with an unknown gating factor, and let the estimator be the least-squares minimizer over all candidate mixing measures with at most an overspecified number of prompt atoms and all admissible gating factors. Define the Voronoi loss between an estimated mixing measure and the true one by assigning each estimated atom to the nearest true atom, summing the mismatch in the total exponential weights inside each Voronoi cell, then adding a first-order displacement penalty when a cell contains exactly one estimated atom and a second-order displacement penalty when a cell contains more than one estimated atom. If the activation function satisfies the stated uniform Lipschitz regularity condition up to second order and is injective, then whenever the number of candidate prompt atoms is strictly larger than the true number of prompt atoms, the least-squares estimator achieves Voronoi-loss error of order sqrt(log n / n) in probability and gating-factor estimation error of order sqrt(log n / n) in probability.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof starts from the previously established parametric convergence rate of the estimated regression function in L2. It then reduces the theorem to proving a quantitative identifiability inequality: the L2 distance between two models must be bounded below by a constant multiple of the sum of the Voronoi loss and the gating-factor error. To show this lower bound, the argument splits into a local part and a global part. In the local part, one assumes for contradiction that there exists a sequence of estimated mixing measures and gating factors whose normalized model error goes to zero even though the normalization by Voronoi loss plus gating error is nontrivial. Around the true prompts, the proof performs first- and second-order Taylor expansions of the non-linear prompt terms and a first-order expansion of the gating term. Using the regularity assumption on the activation, all remainder terms are shown to be negligible after normalization. The normalized discrepancy is then written as a linear combination of explicitly identified linearly independent functions. The next step shows that not all coefficients in this expansion can vanish asymptotically; otherwise, injectivity of the activation and the resulting identifiability force the estimated prompts, weights, and gating factor to collapse to the true parameters, contradicting the normalized setup. After identifying a non-vanishing coefficient, the proof rescales by the maximal coefficient and applies Fatou's lemma to obtain a strictly positive lower bound, again contradicting the assumption that the normalized model error converges to zero. The global part follows the same strategy as in the linear case, using compactness and another Fatou-type argument to rule out sequences that stay away from the truth. Combining this lower bound with the parametric L2 convergence rate yields the stated sqrt(log n / n) convergence rates for the Voronoi loss and the gating factor.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem onZeroInitializedAttention_optimalPrompt_and_gatingFactor_estimation
    {Ω : Type u} [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    {Prompt : Type v} {Feature : Type w}
    (P : MeasureTheory.Measure Ω)
    (σ : Prompt → Feature)
    (VoronoiLoss : MixingMeasure Prompt → MixingMeasure Prompt → ℝ)
    (IsNonlinearZeroInitializedAttentionModel :
      MeasureTheory.Measure Ω → (Prompt → Feature) → MixingMeasure Prompt → ℝ → Prop)
    (IsLeastSquareEstimatorNonlinear :
      (Prompt → Feature) → MixingMeasure Prompt → ℝ →
      (ℕ → Ω → MixingMeasure Prompt) → (ℕ → Ω → ℝ) → Prop)
    (UniformLipschitzActivation : (Prompt → Feature) → Prop)
    (Gstar : MixingMeasure Prompt)
    (αstar : ℝ)
    (Gen : ℕ → Ω → MixingMeasure Prompt)
    (αen : ℕ → Ω → ℝ)
    (L L' : ℕ)
    (hModel : IsNonlinearZeroInitializedAttentionModel P σ Gstar αstar)
    (hOverspec : L < L')
    (hA1 : UniformLipschitzActivation σ)
    (hA2 : Function.Injective σ)
    (hEstimator : IsLeastSquareEstimatorNonlinear σ Gstar αstar Gen αen) :
    IsBigOPInProbability P (fun n ω => VoronoiLoss (Gen n ω) Gstar) parametricRate ∧
      IsBigOPInProbability P (fun n ω => αen n ω - αstar) parametricRate := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

universe u v w

structure MixingMeasure (Prompt : Type u) where
  support : Finset Prompt
  weight : Prompt → ℝ

def parametricRate (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ) / (n : ℝ))

def IsBigOPInProbability
    {Ω : Type u} [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    (P : MeasureTheory.Measure Ω)
    (X : ℕ → Ω → ℝ)
    (r : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n ≥ N,
      P {ω | C * |r n| ≤ |X n ω|} ≤ ENNReal.ofReal ε

metadata.source_paper: Making Transformer Decoders Better Differentiable Indexers

natural_language.core_claim: A Transformer decoder can function as a differentiable indexer: once query-to-bucket distributions have been learned, a simple greedy rule can recover the underlying item-to-bucket allocation with high probability when the number of queries is sufficiently large.
natural_language.informal_theorem:
Fix a tolerance level δ ∈ (0, 1). Consider a dataset D with m queries and N items, where the N items are evenly allocated into k buckets, and each query is related to each item independently with probability p. For every query x, suppose a probability distribution Qx over the k buckets is given. For each item y, let X⁺_y be the set of queries related to y, compute the expected bucket distribution E_{x ∈ X⁺_y}[Qx], and assign y to the bucket whose probability in this expected distribution is maximal. If
m ≥ ([Φ⁻¹(1 - δ)]² · (1 + (2N/k) · p · (1 + p))) / (p · (1 - p)),
then the probability that this greedy procedure successfully recovers the bucket allocation of each item is at least 1 - δ, where Φ⁻¹ is the inverse cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution.
natural_language.informal_proof: Rewrite the allocation-recovery problem in matrix form. Let W be the m × N binary query-item interaction matrix, let H be the N × k binary allocation matrix with exactly one 1 in each row and exactly N/k ones in each column, and let M = W · H. The greedy rule for recovering the bucket of item i compares, for every incorrect bucket j, the score gap Δs_i^(j) between the correct bucket j* and j. This gap can be written as c_{i,i} plus a sum of pairwise co-occurrence terms c_{i,l} weighted by H_{l,j*} - H_{l,j}. Next compute the expectation and variance of c_{i,i} and c_{i,l} using the Bernoulli model for the entries of W, which gives E[c_{i,i}] = mp, Var[c_{i,i}] = mp(1 - p), E[c_{i,l}] = mp², and Var[c_{i,l}] = mp²(1 - p²). From these, derive E[Δs_i^(j)] = mp(1 - p), and bound Var[Δs_i^(j)] by using the balanced-column structure of H, which implies that the number of active disagreement terms is at most 2N/k. Then approximate Δs_i^(j) by a normal random variable via the central limit theorem. The probability of correct assignment is therefore Φ(E[Δs_i^(j)] / sqrt(Var[Δs_i^(j)])). Substituting the variance bound and the lower bound on m yields that this probability is at least 1 - δ, which proves the claimed high-probability recovery guarantee.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem making_transformer_decoders_better_differentiable_indexers_thm1

    {δ p : ℝ}
    (Q : Fin m → Fin k → ℝ)
    (Related : Fin m → Fin N → Prop)
    (bucketOf : Fin N → Fin k)
    (hδ : 0 < δ ∧ δ < 1)
    (hN : 0 < N)
    (hk : 0 < k)
    (hp : 0 < p ∧ p < 1)
    (hQ : ∀ x : Fin m, IsProbabilityVector (Q x))
    (hEven : EvenAllocation bucketOf)
    (hRel : IIDBernoulliRelation Related p)
    (hm :
      (m : ℝ) ≥
        ((StdNormalInvCDF (1 - δ)) ^ 2 *
            (1 + (((2 : ℝ) * (N : ℝ)) / (k : ℝ)) * p * (1 + p))) /
          (p * (1 - p))) :
    GreedyRecoverySuccessProb Q Related bucketOf ≥ 1 - δ := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Positional Attention: Expressivity and Learnability of Algorithmic Computation

natural_language.core_claim: Positional Transformers retain the expressive power needed for algorithmic computation: any MPC computation with R rounds, N machines, and local memory s can be approximated arbitrarily well by a positional Transformer with N + 1 nodes, 2R⌈log N⌉ layers, and 2s attention heads.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider an MPC instance with R rounds, N machines, and local memory size s, in which the local computation performed by each machine is Borel measurable. Consider also a positional Transformer whose layers are built by concatenating the outputs of positional-attention heads, applying an output transformation, concatenating the residual input, and then passing the result through an MLP, where the attention weights depend only on fixed positional encodings and not on the input values. If this model has n equal to N plus 1 nodes, 2R times the ceiling of log base 2 of N layers, and 2s attention heads, then there exists a choice of parameters for this positional Transformer that approximates the given MPC instance to any desired degree of accuracy.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof proceeds in two simulation steps. First, the dynamic communication of the MPC computation is converted into a data-agnostic communication process by introducing a proxy model called Parallel Computation with Oracle Communication. This simulation uses a Beneš-network-style routing scheme, which shows that R rounds of MPC with N machines and local memory s can be simulated using 2R⌈log N⌉ rounds of the proxy model with local memory 2s. Second, one shows that a positional Transformer can simulate one round of this proxy model in one layer: positional attention realizes the fixed communication pattern between nodes, while the MLP and residual pathway realize the local computation at each node. The local computation step is handled by universal approximation results for neural networks, which apply because the local functions are Borel measurable. Composing the two simulations yields a positional Transformer with N + 1 nodes, 2R⌈log N⌉ layers, and 2s attention heads that approximates the original MPC computation arbitrarily well.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem paelac_483271
    (R N s : ℕ)
    (hN : 1 ≤ N)
    (T : MPCInstance)
    (M : PositionalTransformer)
    (h_arch : FollowsEquation1 M)
    (h_rounds : T.rounds = R)
    (h_machines : T.machines = N)
    (h_memory : T.localMemory = s)
    (h_borel : T.borelLocalFunctions)
    (h_nodes : M.nodes = N + 1)
    (h_layers : M.layers = 2 * R * ceilLog2 N)
    (h_heads : M.attentionHeads = 2 * s) :
    ∃ θ : ModelConfig M, ApproximatesToAnyDesiredAccuracy θ T := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

structure MPCInstance where
  rounds : ℕ
  machines : ℕ
  localMemory : ℕ
  borelLocalFunctions : Prop

structure PositionalTransformer where
  nodes : ℕ
  layers : ℕ
  attentionHeads : ℕ

structure ModelConfig (_M : PositionalTransformer) where
  witness : Unit

def FollowsEquation1 (_M : PositionalTransformer) : Prop :=
  True

def ApproximatesToAnyDesiredAccuracy
    {M : PositionalTransformer}
    (_θ : ModelConfig M)
    (_T : MPCInstance) : Prop :=
  ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε → True

def ceilLog2 (N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
  ⌈Real.logb 2 (N : ℝ)⌉₊

metadata.source_paper: Beyond the Permutation Symmetry of Transformers: The Role of Rotation for Model Fusion

natural_language.core_claim: Rotation symmetry turns the optimal matching of corresponding self-attention parameters into a closed-form SVD problem, giving a principled way to align transformers before model fusion.
natural_language.informal_theorem:
Let R denote the set of rotation matrices. Consider the optimization problem
min_{R1,R2∈R} ‖[[W_Q1^⊤, W_Q2^⊤], [b_Q1, b_Q2]] [R1; -R2]‖_F^2 + ‖[[W_K1^⊤, W_K2^⊤], [b_K1, b_K2]] [R1; -R2]‖_F^2.
This optimization problem has the closed-form solution R1 = U V^⊤ and R2 = I, where I is the identity matrix and U Σ V^⊤ is a singular value decomposition of W_Q1 W_Q2^⊤ + W_K1 W_K2^⊤ + b_Q1^⊤ b_Q2 + b_K1^⊤ b_K2.
natural_language.informal_proof: First expand the block-matrix objective into a sum of four Frobenius-norm terms involving W_Q1^⊤ R1 - W_Q2^⊤ R2, b_Q1 R1 - b_Q2 R2, W_K1^⊤ R1 - W_K2^⊤ R2, and b_K1 R1 - b_K2 R2. Then introduce the change of variables R = R1 R2^{-1}. Because right multiplication by a rotation matrix preserves the Frobenius norm, every factor R2 can be removed from the objective, reducing the problem to an Orthogonal Procrustes problem over a single rotation matrix R. Next rewrite the minimization as maximizing a Frobenius inner product between R and the matrix W_Q1 W_Q2^⊤ + W_K1 W_K2^⊤ + b_Q1^⊤ b_Q2 + b_K1^⊤ b_K2. Taking a singular value decomposition U Σ V^⊤ of this matrix, the standard Procrustes argument shows that the maximizer is R = U V^⊤. Finally substitute back R = R1 R2^{-1} and choose the anchor convention R2 = I, which yields the closed-form solution R1 = U V^⊤ and R2 = I.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem bpstrmf_482731
    {n d : ℕ}
    (WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin n) ℝ)
    (bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 : Matrix (Fin 1) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (U Sigma V : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (hU : IsRotation U)
    (hV : IsRotation V)
    (hSigmaDiag : Matrix.IsDiag Sigma)
    (hSigmaNonneg : ∀ i : Fin d, 0 ≤ Sigma i i)
    (hSVD :
      U * Sigma * V.transpose =
        WQ1 * WQ2.transpose +
        WK1 * WK2.transpose +
        bQ1.transpose * bQ2 +
        bK1.transpose * bK2) :
    Theorem41Optimal WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 (U * V.transpose) 1 := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Matrix
open scoped BigOperators

noncomputable section

def IsRotation {d : ℕ} (R : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ) : Prop :=
  R * R.transpose = 1

def frobeniusSq {m n : Type*} [Fintype m] [DecidableEq m] [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n]
    (A : Matrix m n ℝ) : ℝ :=
  ∑ i, ∑ j, (A i j) ^ (2 : ℕ)

def vstackDiff {d : ℕ}
    (R1 R2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ) :
    Matrix (Sum (Fin d) (Fin d)) (Fin d) ℝ :=
  fun i j =>
    match i with
    | Sum.inl i' => R1 i' j
    | Sum.inr i' => -R2 i' j

def theorem41Obj
    {n d : ℕ}
    (WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin n) ℝ)
    (bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 : Matrix (Fin 1) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (R1 R2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ) : ℝ :=
  let AQ : Matrix (Sum (Fin n) (Fin 1)) (Sum (Fin d) (Fin d)) ℝ :=
    Matrix.fromBlocks WQ1.transpose WQ2.transpose bQ1 bQ2
  let AK : Matrix (Sum (Fin n) (Fin 1)) (Sum (Fin d) (Fin d)) ℝ :=
    Matrix.fromBlocks WK1.transpose WK2.transpose bK1 bK2
  frobeniusSq (AQ * vstackDiff R1 R2) +
    frobeniusSq (AK * vstackDiff R1 R2)

def Theorem41Optimal
    {n d : ℕ}
    (WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin n) ℝ)
    (bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 : Matrix (Fin 1) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (R1 R2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ) : Prop :=
  IsRotation R1 ∧
  IsRotation R2 ∧
  ∀ (S1 S2 : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ),
    IsRotation S1 →
    IsRotation S2 →
    theorem41Obj WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 R1 R2 ≤
      theorem41Obj WQ1 WQ2 WK1 WK2 bQ1 bQ2 bK1 bK2 S1 S2

metadata.source_paper: Hierarchical Self-Attention: Generalizing Neural Attention Mechanics to Multi-Scale Problems

natural_language.core_claim: Hierarchical self-attention is the KL-optimal block-structured approximation of flat Softmax attention for nested multi-scale signals.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Assume that the query and key variables are normalized with LayerNorm. Let ψ be the interaction energy, and let Θ be the self-attention matrix obtained for a nested signal from the hierarchical gradient recurrence. Define the temperature by τ = (|ℓ(R_x)| / √d)^(-1), and rescale the attention matrix by Θ̂ = -(1/τ)Θ. Then Θ̂ is a stochastic matrix, meaning that every entry is nonnegative and every row sums to 1. Moreover, among all stochastic attention matrices that satisfy the block constraint induced by the signal hierarchy, Θ̂ minimizes the total row-wise KL divergence to the classical Softmax attention matrix of the flattened signal, where the flattened attention weights are given by θ^f_{i,j} = exp(-ψ_{i→j}) / Σ_{k∈ℓ(R_x), k≠i} exp(-ψ_{i→k}).
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof treats the closest block-constrained approximation as a recursive optimization problem over the hierarchy. It first establishes an optimal-substructure lemma for KL divergence: restricting a KL-optimal categorical distribution to any substructure remains optimal for the corresponding restricted problem. Applied row-wise to the optimal block-constrained attention matrix, this shows that each diagonal block associated with a child subtree must itself be an optimal solution for the corresponding restricted flattened attention matrix. The flattened attention matrix and the unknown block-constrained optimum are then both decomposed into within-subtree and cross-subtree terms. Substituting these decompositions into the total KL objective yields an optimization problem in the coefficients that control intra-block renormalization and inter-block attention. Enforcing row-stochasticity gives a constrained problem, which is solved by forming a Lagrangian and differentiating with respect to those coefficients. The resulting optimal coefficients are expressed through auxiliary quantities γ and ζ. The key final step is to show that γ and ζ satisfy exactly the same recurrence as the hierarchical energy coefficients used by hierarchical self-attention, with the same one-level base case as ordinary Softmax attention. Therefore the attention matrix produced by the hierarchical recurrence coincides with the KL minimizer, proving that hierarchical self-attention is the closest block-constrained approximation to flattened Softmax attention.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem HSAGNAMTMP_482731
    {Idx : Type} [Fintype Idx] [DecidableEq Idx]
    (d tau : ℝ)
    (psi : Idx → Idx → ℝ)
    (Theta ThetaHat ThetaFlat : Matrix Idx Idx ℝ)
    (B : Set (Matrix Idx Idx ℝ))
    (QKLayerNormNormalized : Prop)
    (DerivedFromGradientRecurrence : Matrix Idx Idx ℝ → Prop)
    (HasBlockConstraint : Matrix Idx Idx ℝ → Prop)
    (rowKL : (Idx → ℝ) → (Idx → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (hNorm : QKLayerNormNormalized)
    (hDeriv : DerivedFromGradientRecurrence Theta)
    (hTau : tau = (((Fintype.card Idx : ℝ) / Real.sqrt d))⁻¹)
    (hThetaHat : ThetaHat = (- (1 / tau)) • Theta)
    (hB : B = {Psi | (∀ i j, 0 ≤ Psi i j) ∧ (∀ i, ∑ j, Psi i j = 1) ∧ HasBlockConstraint Psi})
    (hThetaFlat : ∀ i j, ThetaFlat i j = Real.exp (- psi i j) / ∑ k : Idx, if k = i then 0 else Real.exp (- psi i k)) :
    ((∀ i j, 0 ≤ ThetaHat i j) ∧ (∀ i, ∑ j, ThetaHat i j = 1)) ∧
      ∀ Psi, Psi ∈ B →
        ((∑ i : Idx, rowKL (fun j => ThetaHat i j) (fun j => ThetaFlat i j)) ≤
          (∑ i : Idx, rowKL (fun j => Psi i j) (fun j => ThetaFlat i j))) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

metadata.source_paper: The Non-Linear Representation Dilemma: Is Causal Abstraction Enough for Mechanistic Interpretability?

natural_language.core_claim: Without restricting the complexity of alignment maps, causal abstraction becomes vacuous in the sense that, under reasonable assumptions, any algorithm can be realized as an input-restricted distributed abstraction of any neural network.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider any task, any algorithm for that task, and any neural network for the same task. Assume that the input space is countable; that for every layer the mapping obtained by running the network from the input up to that layer is injective; that for every layer the composition of the network from that layer to the output with the output abstraction map is strictly surjective, meaning that every output label can be made the unique argmax at some hidden state; that the network’s inner neurons can be partitioned into single-neuron blocks indexed by the algorithm’s internal nodes in a way that respects the algorithm’s partial ordering and leaves at least one neuron unused in every layer; and that the neural network solves the task correctly on every input. Then there exists an abstraction map such that the algorithm is an input-restricted abstraction of the neural network and this abstraction map is distributed. Equivalently, the algorithm is an input-restricted distributed abstraction of the neural network.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof begins by unpacking what must be shown from the definition of input-restricted distributed abstraction: it is enough to construct an abstraction map that is both distributed and yields an input-restricted abstraction. The construction is then organized around three objects: a family of layer-wise alignment maps, a partition of the aligned hidden variables, and a family of per-node subabstraction maps. Assumption 4 guarantees a fixed partition that matches the algorithm’s partial order, assigns exactly one aligned neuron to each algorithmic node, and leaves spare neurons in every layer. Using this partition, the argument proceeds by induction over layers. At each layer, the construction maintains the invariant that the value of every algorithmic node assigned to that layer can be recovered from its aligned hidden variable under any input-restricted intervention from previous layers. Input-injectivity is used to recover the relevant input information from intermediate hidden states, strict output-surjectivity is used to choose hidden states that force the required downstream outputs, and the unused neuron in each layer is used to carry the necessary information forward without violating the partition constraints. At the final layer, correctness of the neural network on the task ensures that the output node recovered by the abstraction agrees with the task output. The induction therefore produces a distributed abstraction map witnessing that the algorithm is an input-restricted distributed abstraction of the neural network.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem nonLinearRepresentationDilemma_theorem1
    {X Y : Type u}
    (task : X → Y)
    (A : Algorithm X Y)
    (N : NeuralNetwork X Y)
    (InputInjectiveInAllLayers : NeuralNetwork X Y → Prop)
    (StrictOutputSurjectiveInAllLayers : NeuralNetwork X Y → Prop)
    (MatchablePartialOrderings : Algorithm X Y → NeuralNetwork X Y → Prop)
    (DnnSolvesTask : (X → Y) → NeuralNetwork X Y → Prop)
    (IsInputRestrictedTauAbstraction :
      (A : Algorithm X Y) → (N : NeuralNetwork X Y) → AbstractionMap A N → Prop)
    (IsDistributedAbstractionMap :
      (A : Algorithm X Y) → (N : NeuralNetwork X Y) → AbstractionMap A N → Prop)
    (h_countable_input_space : Countable X)
    (h_input_injective_in_all_layers : InputInjectiveInAllLayers N)
    (h_strict_output_surjective_in_all_layers : StrictOutputSurjectiveInAllLayers N)
    (h_matchable_partial_orderings : MatchablePartialOrderings A N)
    (h_dnn_solves_task : DnnSolvesTask task N) :
    ∃ τ : AbstractionMap A N,
      IsInputRestrictedTauAbstraction A N τ ∧
      IsDistributedAbstractionMap A N τ := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

universe u

structure Algorithm (X Y : Type u) where
  dummy : Unit := ()

structure NeuralNetwork (X Y : Type u) where
  dummy : Unit := ()

structure AbstractionMap {X Y : Type u} (A : Algorithm X Y) (N : NeuralNetwork X Y) where
  dummy : Unit := ()

metadata.source_paper: Emergence of meta-stable clustering in mean-field transformer models

natural_language.core_claim: From uniform random initialization, mean-field transformer dynamics enter a meta-stable phase in which the empirical token distribution stays close to a structured k_max-periodic nonlinear mode and remains a fixed positive distance away from the uniform measure.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the mean-field transformer dynamics in dimension two, with the circle identified with the interval from zero to two pi. Initialize the system with N tokens drawn independently and uniformly at random on the circle, let mu_t be the empirical measure, let mu_infinity be the uniform measure, and define the perturbation rho_t as mu_t minus mu_infinity. Let T1 be the linear-phase time given by one over gamma_max times the logarithm of N to the power minus one fourth divided by the H minus one norm of rho_0. Let alpha be N to the power minus one fourth times the Fourier coefficient of rho_0 with index k_max divided by the H minus one norm of rho_0, and let f_alpha be the solution of the mean-field PDE with initial data mu_infinity plus alpha times cosine of k_max theta. For a fixed parameter delta greater than zero and small enough, define T2 as one over gamma_max times the logarithm of delta divided by alpha. Then, as N tends to infinity, the Wasserstein-1 distance between mu evaluated at time T1 plus T2 and f_alpha evaluated at time T2 converges to zero in probability, and the Wasserstein-1 distance between mu evaluated at time T1 plus T2 and the uniform measure is strictly larger than delta.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument first analyzes the linearized dynamics around the uniform measure and shows that, among all unstable Fourier modes, the mode with index k_max is the dominant one on the time scale up to T1. A duality-based estimate for the linearized operator improves the effective Lyapunov control and yields a propagation-of-chaos estimate strong enough to justify replacing the particle system by the linear dominant mode at the end of the linear phase. Starting from that time, the proof builds a genuinely nonlinear comparison solution f_alpha generated by the dominant mode. The main difficulty is that direct nonlinear growth estimates are too weak to control the dynamics up to the exit time of the quasi-linear phase, so a higher-order Grenier expansion is introduced to obtain a more accurate approximation. This shows that the empirical measure stays close to f_alpha throughout the quasi-linear phase. Finally, a quantitative lower bound for the distance between f_alpha at time T2 and the uniform measure implies that, at the end of the quasi-linear phase, the empirical measure is still separated from equilibrium by at least delta, while its Wasserstein-1 distance to f_alpha(T2) vanishes in probability.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem emscmftm_482731
    (D : QuasiLinearPhaseData)
    (hgamma : 0 < D.gammaMax)
    (hdelta : 0 < D.delta)
    (hdeltaSmall : SmallEnough D.delta)
    (hT2 : ∀ N : ℕ, D.T2 N = (1 / D.gammaMax) * Real.log (D.delta / D.alpha N))
    (hsol : SolvesIVP6 D) :
    ConvergesInProbabilityToZero
      (fun N : ℕ => D.W1 (D.mu N (D.T1 N + D.T2 N)) (D.fAlpha N (D.T2 N))) ∧
    AwayFromUniformEventually D := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

abbrev Time := ℝ

structure QuasiLinearPhaseData where
  State : Type
  muInf : State
  mu : ℕ → Time → State
  fAlpha : ℕ → Time → State
  W1 : State → State → ℝ
  gammaMax : ℝ
  alpha : ℕ → ℝ
  delta : ℝ
  T1 : ℕ → Time
  T2 : ℕ → Time

def SmallEnough (_ : ℝ) : Prop := True

def SolvesIVP6 (_ : QuasiLinearPhaseData) : Prop := True

def ConvergesInProbabilityToZero (_ : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop := True

def AwayFromUniformEventually (D : QuasiLinearPhaseData) : Prop :=
  ∃ N0 : ℕ, ∀ N ≥ N0, D.W1 (D.mu N (D.T1 N + D.T2 N)) D.muInf > D.delta

metadata.source_paper: A multiscale analysis of mean-field transformers in the moderate interaction regime

natural_language.core_claim: In the large-interaction limit, the alignment phase of a mean-field transformer is governed by a deterministic transport equation on the sphere.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let Q, K, and V be invertible square matrices. Let the initial probability measure on the unit sphere be absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure, and assume that its density is bounded above, bounded away from zero, and Lipschitz continuous. For each inverse-temperature level, let μβ be the solution of the mean-field continuity equation associated with the transformer dynamics. Then, on every fixed finite time interval, the family {μβ} converges in the space of continuous paths of probability measures on the sphere to a limit trajectory μ∞, and this limit trajectory solves the transport equation whose velocity at a point x is the tangential projection of V K transpose Q x divided by the norm of K transpose Q x, with initial datum μ0.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument proceeds in three steps. First, one proves relative compactness of the trajectories μβ in the path space of probability measures by combining a variant of the Ascoli-Arzelà theorem with boundedness of the vector field. Second, one derives estimates that are uniform in the interaction parameter for the regularity of the vector field along the solution trajectories. This is done by analyzing how the kernel exp of beta times the query-key pairing concentrates as beta tends to infinity, using cumulant properties of the Von Mises-Fisher distribution and a continuation argument to propagate regularity in time. Third, compactness and these uniform regularity bounds allow passage to the limit in the weak formulation of the continuity equation, which identifies the limit trajectory with the transport equation above. Uniqueness of the limit equation then yields convergence of the whole family.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem MAMFTMIR_250937
    (D : AlignmentPhaseData)
    (hA1 : D.assumptionsInvertible)
    (hA2 : D.assumptionsInitialDensity)
    (hsol : ∀ β : ℕ, D.solvesContinuityEquation (D.muBeta β)) :
    D.convergesInCPathSpace D.T D.muBeta D.muInf ∧
      D.solvesAlignmentLimitPDE D.muInf D.alignmentDrift D.mu0 := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

universe u v

/-- Abstract package for the alignment-phase convergence statement.
The analytic notions are kept abstract so the theorem remains type-checkable
without rebuilding the full PDE and probability-measure infrastructure. -/
structure AlignmentPhaseData where
  State : Type u
  Curve : Type v
  T : ℝ
  Q : Type u
  K : Type u
  V : Type u
  mu0 : State → ℝ
  muBeta : ℕ → Curve
  muInf : Curve
  /-- Assumption 1: Q, K, and V are invertible square matrices. -/
  assumptionsInvertible : Prop
  /-- Assumption 2: the initial probability measure on the sphere is absolutely
  continuous with bounded, positive, Lipschitz density. -/
  assumptionsInitialDensity : Prop
  /-- Solutions of the original continuity equation. -/
  solvesContinuityEquation : Curve → Prop
  /-- The alignment-phase drift x ↦ P_x (V Kᵀ Q x / ‖Kᵀ Q x‖). -/
  alignmentDrift : State → State
  /-- Convergence in C([0, T], P(S^(d-1))). -/
  convergesInCPathSpace : ℝ → (ℕ → Curve) → Curve → Prop
  /-- Solves ∂ₜ μ(x) = - div (μ(x) • alignmentDrift x) with initial datum mu0. -/
  solvesAlignmentLimitPDE : Curve → (State → State) → (State → ℝ) → Prop

metadata.source_paper: Transformative or Conservative? Conservation laws for ResNets and Transformers

natural_language.core_claim: In gradient-flow training with weight decay, every conservation law is exactly a time-independent conservation law of the corresponding system without weight decay, evaluated at an exponentially rescaled parameter vector.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the gradient-flow dynamics in which the parameter vector evolves according to: its time derivative plus a nonnegative weight-decay schedule times the parameter vector equals the negative empirical-loss gradient for the chosen data set. Let h be a conserved function for this dynamics. Assume that for every parameter vector there exists some data set whose empirical-loss gradient vanishes at that parameter vector. Define H on the parameter space by H(a) = h(0, a). Then for every time and every parameter vector, h at that time and parameter equals H evaluated at the parameter vector multiplied by the exponential of the integral of the weight-decay schedule from zero to that time. Consequently, every conserved function can be expressed using only the parameter variables rather than both time and parameter variables. Moreover, the time-independent function obtained from H is itself a conservation law for the same gradient-flow system without weight decay.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins from the orthogonality characterization of conservation laws: the gradient of a conserved function must be orthogonal to every vector field generated by the training dynamics. Using the assumption that every parameter vector is a stationary point for some data set, one may specialize this characterization to the pure weight-decay direction, which shows that the conserved function is also preserved along the auxiliary flow where only weight decay acts. Solving this auxiliary differential equation explicitly gives an exponential rescaling of the parameter vector, and conservation along that flow yields the representation of h in terms of H(a) = h(0, a). To prove the second part, one differentiates this representation, substitutes it back into the orthogonality condition for the original dynamics with an arbitrary data set, and then evaluates the resulting identity at time zero. This shows that the gradient of H is orthogonal to the gradient-flow direction without weight decay, so the time-independent function induced by H is a conservation law for the zero-weight-decay system.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem transformative_or_conservative_structure_theorem
    (gradL : Ζ → Param ι → Param ι)
    (lam : ℝ → ℝ)
    (h : ℝ → Param ι → ℝ)
    (hlam : ∀ t : ℝ, 0 ≤ lam t)
    (hh : IsConservedWD (ι := ι) gradL lam h)
    (hstationary : ∀ θ : Param ι, ∃ Z : Ζ, gradL Z θ = 0) :
    (∀ t : ℝ, ∀ θ : Param ι,
      h t θ = (fun a : Param ι => h 0 a) ((wdScale lam t) • θ)) ∧
      IsConservedNoWD (ι := ι) gradL (fun a : Param ι => h 0 a) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open Real

noncomputable section

variable {ι Ζ : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι]

abbrev Param (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] := EuclideanSpace ℝ ι

def wdScale (lam : ℝ → ℝ) (t : ℝ) : ℝ :=
  Real.exp (∫ s in (0 : ℝ)..t, lam s)

def IsWDSolution
    (gradL : Ζ → Param ι → Param ι)
    (lam : ℝ → ℝ)
    (Z : Ζ)
    (θ : ℝ → Param ι) : Prop :=
  ∀ t : ℝ, HasDerivAt θ (-(gradL Z (θ t)) - (lam t) • θ t) t

def IsConservedWD
    (gradL : Ζ → Param ι → Param ι)
    (lam : ℝ → ℝ)
    (h : ℝ → Param ι → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ (Z : Ζ) (θ : ℝ → Param ι),
    IsWDSolution (ι := ι) gradL lam Z θ →
    ∀ t : ℝ, h t (θ t) = h 0 (θ 0)

def IsNoWDSolution
    (gradL : Ζ → Param ι → Param ι)
    (Z : Ζ)
    (θ : ℝ → Param ι) : Prop :=
  ∀ t : ℝ, HasDerivAt θ (-(gradL Z (θ t))) t

def IsConservedNoWD
    (gradL : Ζ → Param ι → Param ι)
    (H : Param ι → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ (Z : Ζ) (θ : ℝ → Param ι),
    IsNoWDSolution (ι := ι) gradL Z θ →
    ∀ t : ℝ, H (θ t) = H (θ 0)

metadata.source_paper: The underlying structures of self-attention: symmetry, directionality, and emergent dynamics in Transformer training

natural_language.core_claim: Autoregressive training makes the self-attention query-key matrix develop directional structure by amplifying columns more strongly than rows.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a vocabulary of tokens and a probability distribution over sequences of a fixed length. Let a random sequence be drawn from this distribution, let its token embeddings be sampled independently from a zero-mean distribution with covariance matrix Sigma, and let the query-key matrix of a self-attention mechanism be updated according to the implicit gradient formula associated with autoregressive training. Assume that token occurrences are statistically correlated so that embeddings are only partially aligned, that the entries of the query-key matrix are independently initialized with finite mean and variance, and that some tokens tend to appear earlier in the sequence and are more predictive of later tokens. Then, for every column index and every row index whose corresponding diagonal entry of Sigma is smaller than the average diagonal mass of Sigma, the expected squared norm of the update to that column divided by the expected squared norm of the update to that row is strictly greater than one. Moreover, there exists a positive threshold proportional to the product of the variances of row norms and column norms such that, above this threshold, a column of the query-key matrix is more likely than a row to have squared norm larger than the threshold. In other words, autoregressive training makes high-norm columns more likely than high-norm rows, yielding a directional bias in the query-key matrix.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins by expanding the update of the query-key matrix into tokenwise contributions and then using the row-versus-column decomposition for a fixed token. This yields explicit dataset-level formulas for the update of a chosen column and a chosen row, and from these formulas one derives an expression for the ratio between their expected squared update norms. The next step is to analyze how often a token acts as context versus how often it is predicted under an autoregressive objective. Because earlier tokens are assumed to be more predictive of future content, the expected number of times such a token appears as context is larger than the expected number of times it is itself predicted. Substituting this imbalance into the ratio formula shows that the expected squared update norm of a column exceeds that of a row whenever the relevant diagonal covariance entry is below the average diagonal mass. Since the query-key matrix is initialized with independent entries, row norms and column norms start with equal variance, but the larger column updates make the variance of column norms become larger during training. A tail-comparison lemma for distributions with equal mean and different variance then implies that beyond a positive threshold proportional to the product of the two variances, the probability that a column norm exceeds the threshold is larger than the corresponding probability for a row norm. This establishes directional bias.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem underlying_structures_autoregressive_training_induces_directionality
    {Token : Type}
    {d : ℕ}
    [Fact (0 < d)]
    (tStar : Token)
    (Wqk deltaWqk : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (SigmaDiag : Fin d → ℝ)
    (trSigma : ℝ)
    (ED_colUpdateSq ED_rowUpdateSq : Fin d → ℝ)
    (Pr_colNormSq_gt Pr_rowNormSq_gt : ℝ → ℝ)
    (rowNormVar colNormVar : ℝ)
    (AutoregressiveObjective GradientUpdateAsInProposition21
      EmbeddingsIIDZeroMeanCovariance TokensStatisticallyCorrelated
      IIDInitializationFiniteMeanVariance EarlyTokenBias : Prop) :
    AutoregressiveObjective →
    GradientUpdateAsInProposition21 →
    EmbeddingsIIDZeroMeanCovariance →
    TokensStatisticallyCorrelated →
    IIDInitializationFiniteMeanVariance →
    EarlyTokenBias →
    ((∀ k m : Fin d,
        SigmaDiag m < trSigma / (d : ℝ) →
          ED_colUpdateSq k / ED_rowUpdateSq m > 1) ∧
      ∃ gamma : ℝ,
        0 < gamma ∧
        (∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ gamma = c * rowNormVar * colNormVar) ∧
        ∀ w : ℝ, gamma < w → Pr_colNormSq_gt w > Pr_rowNormSq_gt w) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: A unified framework for establishing the universal approximation of transformer-type architectures

natural_language.core_claim: A transformer-type architecture is a universal approximator of continuous equivariant sequence-to-sequence functions once its token-wise feedforward family is nonlinear and affine-invariant and its token-mixing family can distinguish tokens up to the underlying group action.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Assume that the token dimension is at least two and that the zero token-mixing map belongs to the token-mixing family. Let a transformer family be generated by composing residual token-mixing maps from a family of token-mixing operators with residual token-wise feedforward maps induced by a family of single-token maps. Suppose the single-token family is nonlinear and affine-invariant, in the sense that it is closed under affine changes of variables and output linear maps and contains at least one non-affine Lipschitz map. Suppose also that the token-mixing family satisfies token distinguishability under a subgroup action on token positions, meaning that for every finite set of samples in general position there exists a finite composition of residual token-mixing maps such that any two samples from different group orbits are sent to outputs whose tokens are all pairwise distinct. Then the resulting transformer family has the group-universal approximation property in the Lp sense for every p between one and infinity: every continuous equivariant map on token arrays can be approximated arbitrarily well on every compact set by a transformer from this family.
natural_language.informal_proof: Fix a continuous equivariant target map on a compact domain and replace the compact set by a hypercube. First approximate the target in Lp by a piecewise-constant map on a sufficiently fine grid. Next use the interpolation property implied by token distinguishability together with the expressive token-wise feedforward family to construct a transformer that matches the target values at grid corner points in general position and remains uniformly bounded at the exceptional corner points. Then build a continuous one-dimensional map that collapses each shrunken grid interval to its left endpoint and extend it coordinatewise to token arrays; by the tensor-type approximation result, this coordinatewise collapsing map can itself be approximated by a transformer from the same family. Composing the two transformers yields a model that is almost constant on each shrunken grid cell and therefore reproduces the piecewise-constant approximation on the good cells. The contribution of cells whose corner points are not in general position is controlled because their total measure is small, the boundary strip between the shrunken cells and the full cells is controlled by choosing the shrinkage parameter close to one, and the replacement of the collapsing map by its transformer approximation is controlled by uniform continuity. Summing these error terms gives an arbitrarily small total Lp error.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem ufeuata_583214
    {d n : ℕ}
    (hd : 2 ≤ d)
    (H : Set (FeedforwardMap d))
    (Gmix : Set (TokenMixingMap d n))
    (TG_H : Set (ModelMap d n))
    (OrbitData : Type)
    (orbit : OrbitData)
    (zero_mem_Gmix : (fun _ => 0) ∈ Gmix)
    (NonlinearAffineInvariant : Set (FeedforwardMap d) → Prop)
    (TokenDistinguishability : Set (TokenMixingMap d n) → OrbitData → Prop)
    (GUAP : Set (ModelMap d n) → OrbitData → Prop)
    (hH : NonlinearAffineInvariant H)
    (hG : TokenDistinguishability Gmix orbit) :
    GUAP TG_H orbit := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

abbrev Token (d : ℕ) := Fin d → ℝ
abbrev TokenArray (d n : ℕ) := Fin n → Token d
abbrev FeedforwardMap (d : ℕ) := Token d → Token d
abbrev TokenMixingMap (d n : ℕ) := TokenArray d n → TokenArray d n
abbrev ModelMap (d n : ℕ) := TokenArray d n → TokenArray d n

metadata.source_paper: Limits of Deep Learning: Sequence Modeling through the Lens of Complexity Theory

natural_language.core_claim: A finite-precision structured state space model is effectively finite-state, so the language it recognizes is always regular.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a finite-precision structured state space model, and define its language as the set of all input sequences for which the final hidden state is nonzero. Then the language recognized by this model belongs to the class of regular languages.
natural_language.informal_proof: Because the model has finite precision and fixed hidden dimension, the set of all possible hidden states is finite. Construct a finite-state machine whose states are exactly these possible hidden vectors, whose input alphabet is the corresponding finite token set, whose transition function is given by the model’s state update rule, whose start state is the initial hidden state, and whose accepting states are precisely all nonzero hidden states. This automaton reproduces the same state evolution as the model on every input sequence and therefore accepts exactly the same sequences. Hence the language recognized by the finite-precision model is regular.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem ldlsmlct_483271
    (M : FinitePrecisionSSM) :
    RegularLanguage (FinitePrecisionSSM.language M) := by
  refine ⟨
    { State := M.State
      instFintypeState := M.instFintypeState
      instDecidableEqState := M.instDecidableEqState
      start := M.h0
      step := M.step
      accept := { s | s ≠ M.zero } },
    ?_⟩
  intro w
  rfl
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

universe u

abbrev Lang (α : Type u) := List α → Prop

structure SimpleDFA (α : Type u) where
  State : Type u
  instFintypeState : Fintype State
  instDecidableEqState : DecidableEq State
  start : State
  step : State → α → State
  accept : Set State

attribute [instance] SimpleDFA.instFintypeState SimpleDFA.instDecidableEqState

def SimpleDFA.Accepts {α : Type u} (M : SimpleDFA α) (w : List α) : Prop :=
  List.foldl M.step M.start w ∈ M.accept

def RegularLanguage {α : Type u} (L : Lang α) : Prop :=
  ∃ M : SimpleDFA α, ∀ w : List α, L w ↔ M.Accepts w

structure FinitePrecisionSSM where
  State : Type u
  instFintypeState : Fintype State
  instDecidableEqState : DecidableEq State
  h0 : State
  step : State → State → State
  zero : State

attribute [instance] FinitePrecisionSSM.instFintypeState FinitePrecisionSSM.instDecidableEqState

def FinitePrecisionSSM.Accepts (M : FinitePrecisionSSM) (w : List M.State) : Prop :=
  List.foldl M.step M.h0 w ≠ M.zero

def FinitePrecisionSSM.language (M : FinitePrecisionSSM) : Lang M.State :=
  fun w => M.Accepts w

metadata.source_paper: On the Optimization and Generalization of Two-layer Transformers with Sign Gradient Descent

natural_language.core_claim: Under strong sparse noise, Sign Gradient Descent drives a two-layer transformer to near-zero training loss in logarithmic time while its test loss remains constant-order because the model memorizes training noise and shifts attention onto the noise patch.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a binary classification problem in dimension d in which each example consists of two patches: one randomly chosen patch is the signal patch equal to the label times a fixed signal vector, and the other is a sparse noise patch whose nonzero entries are Gaussian with standard deviation sigma_p on s randomly selected coordinates outside the signal coordinate. Train a two-layer transformer with a single-head softmax attention layer with trainable query, key, and value parameters, followed by a fixed-sign linear head, by Sign Gradient Descent on the logistic loss from Gaussian initialization. Assume that the data dimension is sufficiently large with d growing polynomially in the sample size, the sparsity level satisfies s = Theta(d^(1/2) n^(-2)), the noise strength satisfies sigma_p = Omega(d^(-1/4) n^3), the query-key width and value width are both at least polylogarithmic in d, the initialization scale satisfies sigma_0 = o(sigma_p^(-1) s^(-1) m_k^(-1/2)), the sample size satisfies n = Omega(m_k^4), and the learning rate is sufficiently small with eta = O(poly(d^(-1))). Then for every positive epsilon, with probability at least one minus n^(-1/3), there exist training times T = O(log(epsilon^(-1)) eta^(-1) sigma_p^(-1) s^(-1)) and T_attn = O~(eta^(-1) m_k^(-1/2) sigma_p^(-1/2) s^(-1/2) ||mu||^(-1/2)) such that the training loss satisfies L_S(W(T)) <= epsilon, the test loss satisfies L_D(W(T)) = Theta(1), the mean value vector memorizes the training noise in the sense that for every training example i one has |<v(T), xi_i>| = Omega(1) while |<v(T), mu>| = O~(sigma_p^(-1) s^(-1)), and the attention layer asymptotically places all its weight on the noise patch for every training example, namely s_{i,11}(T_attn) = o(1) and s_{i,21}(T_attn) = o(1).
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof decomposes the final claim into two already established ingredients. First, the analysis of the value dynamics shows that the mean value vector keeps growing along the memorized training-noise directions, so the margin on every training example becomes at least logarithmic in epsilon^(-1), which yields training loss at most epsilon. At the same time, for a fresh test example, with high probability its sparse noise support is disjoint from the training noise supports, so the memorized noise does not transfer; the remaining signal contribution is only constant-order, which forces the logistic test loss to stay constant-order instead of vanishing. Second, the Stage IV analysis of the attention layer shows that after the sign-alignment process between query, key, signal, and noise components, the signal-signal and noise-signal softmax outputs decay to zero, meaning that the attention matrix concentrates asymptotically all its mass on the noise patch. Combining the training-loss and test-loss lemma with the attention-memorization lemma yields the full statement.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem OGTLTSGD_531204
    (n mk mv : ℕ)
    (ε η σp s μnorm : ℝ)
    (Cond31 : Prop)
    (ProbAtLeast : Prop → ℝ → Prop)
    (BigO TildeBigO BigOmega BigTheta : ℝ → ℝ → Prop)
    (LittleOOne : ℝ → Prop)
    (trainLoss testLoss valueSignal : ℕ → ℝ)
    (valueNoise : Fin n → ℕ → ℝ)
    (signalSignalSoftmax noiseSignalSoftmax : Fin n → ℕ → ℝ)
    (hn : 0 < n)
    (hmk : 0 < mk)
    (hmv : 0 < mv)
    (hε : 0 < ε)
    (hη : 0 < η)
    (hσp : 0 < σp)
    (hs : 0 < s)
    (hμ : 0 < μnorm)
    (hCond : Cond31) :
    ∃ E : Prop,
      ProbAtLeast E (1 - Real.rpow (n : ℝ) (-(1 : ℝ) / 3)) ∧
      (E →
        ∃ T Tattn : ℕ,
          BigO (T : ℝ) (Real.log (ε⁻¹) * η⁻¹ * σp⁻¹ * s⁻¹) ∧
          TildeBigO
            (Tattn : ℝ)
            (η⁻¹
              * Real.rpow (mk : ℝ) (-(1 : ℝ) / 2)
              * Real.rpow σp (-(1 : ℝ) / 2)
              * Real.rpow s (-(1 : ℝ) / 2)
              * Real.rpow μnorm (-(1 : ℝ) / 2)) ∧
          trainLoss T ≤ ε ∧
          BigTheta (testLoss T) (1 : ℝ) ∧
          (∀ i : Fin n, BigOmega (|valueNoise i T|) (1 : ℝ)) ∧
          TildeBigO (|valueSignal T|) (σp⁻¹ * s⁻¹) ∧
          (∀ i : Fin n,
            LittleOOne (signalSignalSoftmax i Tattn) ∧
            LittleOOne (noiseSignalSoftmax i Tattn))) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Degrees of Freedom for Linear Attention: Distilling Softmax Attention with Optimal Feature Efficiency

natural_language.core_claim: Finite-feature linear attention can distill a target softmax-attention kernel with a high-probability squared L2 error bound controlled by an input-distribution-dependent complexity term, so the required feature count is determined by the effective complexity of each layer rather than by a uniform fixed dimension.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let K be a continuous positive definite kernel on R^d that admits a feature representation K(x, y) = E_{z∼τ}[φ(x; z)φ(y; z)]. Let ρ be an input distribution such that x ↦ K(x, x) is integrable under ρ and (x, z) ↦ φ(x; z) is square-integrable under ρ ⊗ τ. Let Σ : L2(ρ) → L2(ρ) be the integral operator defined by (Σf)(x) = ⟨K(x, ·), f⟩_{L2(ρ)}, and for a sampling density q with respect to τ define N_{q,λ} to be the supremum over z of q(z)^{-1}⟨φ(·; z), (Σ + λI)^{-1}φ(·; z)⟩_{L2(ρ)}. Draw z1, ..., zM independently from q dτ and define the approximate kernel K-hat by K-hat(x, y) = (1 / M) Σ_{m=1}^M q(zm)^{-1} φ(x; zm)φ(y; zm). Then for any δ in (0, 1), any λ > 0, and any t in (0, 3], if M is at least (4 N_{q,λ} / t) log(64 N_{q,λ} / (δ t)), then with probability at least 1 - 2δ the squared L2(ρ ⊗ ρ) error between K and K-hat is bounded by λ t δ^{-1} C_K^(1) + t^2 C_K^(2), where C_K^(1) and C_K^(2) are constants depending only on K.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument starts from the operator-level concentration result used in Bach's kernel-approximation analysis, which controls the preconditioned empirical error Δ_λ = (Σ + λI)^(-1/2)(Σ - Σ-hat)(Σ + λI)^(-1/2) with high probability. On this event, the empirical operator error is sandwiched by ±t(Σ + λI), which gives both an operator-norm bound and a positive-semidefinite comparison for Σ - Σ-hat. The proof then converts the kernel approximation error into an operator quantity by identifying the squared L2(ρ ⊗ ρ) norm of K - K-hat with the Hilbert-Schmidt norm of Σ - Σ-hat, and then with a trace expression involving (Σ - Σ-hat)^2. Expanding this trace and using the operator comparison yields an upper bound in terms of λ times trace terms plus t^2 times a trace-operator-norm term. Finally, the trace of Σ is controlled by the integrability of K(x, x), the trace of Σ-hat is bounded by Markov's inequality, and the resulting coefficients are absorbed into constants C_K^(1) and C_K^(2), producing the stated high-probability error bound.

import Mathlib

open MeasureTheory
open scoped ENNReal

noncomputable section

theorem degrees_of_freedom_for_linear_attention_theorem3_i

    {rho : Measure X}
    {K : X → X → ℝ}
    {Khat : Ω → X → X → ℝ}
    {δ lam t NqLam CK1 CK2 : ℝ}
    {M : ℕ}
    (hδ0 : 0 < δ)
    (hδ1 : δ < 1)
    (hlam : 0 < lam)
    (ht0 : 0 < t)
    (ht3 : t ≤ 3)
    (hM : (4 * NqLam / t) * Real.log (64 * NqLam / (δ * t)) ≤ (M : ℝ)) :
    ENNReal.ofReal (1 - 2 * δ) ≤
      P {ω | (((eLpNorm
        (fun p : X × X => K p.1 p.2 - Khat ω p.1 p.2)
        (2 : ℝ≥0∞)
        (rho.prod rho)).toReal) ^ 2)
          ≤ lam * t / δ * CK1 + t ^ 2 * CK2} := by

metadata.source_paper: Global Optimality of In-context Markovian Dynamics Learning

natural_language.core_claim: For arbitrary-length in-context Markov chains, the true global optimum of the original transformer loss is sandwiched between the minimum of a strictly convex reparameterized objective and the value obtained by projecting that reparameterized minimizer back to the model parameter space.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let phi be the reparameterization from the model parameter space to R^(d m), and let f_tilde : R^(d m) -> R be the reparameterized objective defined by f_tilde(phi(P, Q)) = f(P, Q). Let X* be the global minimizer of f_tilde. Define psi(X) = argmin_(P,Q) ||phi(P, Q) - X||_2^2, namely the parameter choice whose image under phi is closest to X; when a preimage exists, psi(X) is that preimage. Let f* be the global minimum of f. Then f_tilde(X*) <= f* <= f(psi(X*)).
natural_language.informal_proof: Let (P*, Q*) be a global minimizer of the original objective, so f(P*, Q*) = f*. Because the reparameterized objective f_tilde is strictly convex in X, its global minimizer X* achieves the smallest possible value of f_tilde on R^(d m). Therefore f_tilde(X*) <= f_tilde(phi(P*, Q*)) = f(P*, Q*) = f*. This gives the lower bound. For the upper bound, f* is the global minimum of the original objective, so it is no larger than f(P, Q) for any parameter choice. In particular, taking the specific choice (P, Q) = psi(X*) yields f* <= f(psi(X*)). Combining the two inequalities gives the claim.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem GOIMDL_481372
    {d m : ℕ}
    {Param : Type*}
    (phi : Param → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)))
    (psi : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)) → Param)
    (f : Param → ℝ)
    (ftilde : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)) → ℝ)
    (Xstar : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)))
    (fstar : ℝ)
    (hftilde : ∀ p : Param, ftilde (phi p) = f p)
    (hXstar : ∀ X : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)), ftilde Xstar ≤ ftilde X)
    (hfstar_lower : ∀ p : Param, fstar ≤ f p)
    (hfstar_eq : ∃ pstar : Param, f pstar = fstar)
    (hpsi : ∀ X : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin (d * m)), ∀ p : Param,
      ‖phi (psi X) - X‖ ≤ ‖phi p - X‖) :
    ftilde Xstar ≤ fstar ∧ fstar ≤ f (psi Xstar) := by
  sorry
