metadata.source_paper: On the Role of Depth and Looping for In-Context Learning with Task Diversity

natural_language.core_claim: Task diversity imposes an unavoidable depth requirement on in-context linear regression: if the covariance spectrum ranges from α to β, then any Transformer with at most about sqrt(β/α) layers in the restricted-attention setting, or only a sufficiently small constant times log(β/α) layers in the unrestricted setting, must suffer constant error on some normal instance.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the multilayer Transformer whose hidden states are updated by the linear-attention recurrence and whose prediction is the negative of the bottom-right entry after L layers, with activation either ReLU or the identity map. In the restricted-attention case, each matrix Q at layer t has only a top-left block A at layer t and zeros elsewhere, while each matrix P at layer t has only its last row possibly nonzero, namely a row vector u at layer t together with bottom-right entry one. Let S_{α,β} be the set of realizable linear-regression instances whose covariance matrix lies between αI and βI. Then, if L is at most sqrt(β/α), for every choice of restricted weights and every query vector x_q, there exists an instance in S_{α,β} with that same query vector such that the relative prediction error of the Transformer is at least one half. Moreover, if the attention weights are unrestricted, then whenever L is only a sufficiently small constant times log(β/α), there still exists some instance in S_{α,β} on which the Transformer incurs constant error.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument reduces depth to polynomial degree. In the restricted-attention case, one rescales the feature matrix as X = γU and the query as x_q = θx, and then proves by induction over layers that the prediction coordinate y_q at depth t is a polynomial in the scaling parameter γ of degree at most 2t, with zero value at γ = 0; the same induction also shows that each coordinate of the label state y at depth t has degree at most 2t + 1. The inductive step uses the explicit restricted-attention recurrence together with the positive homogeneity of ReLU, or linearity in the linear-attention case, to control how much the degree can increase from one layer to the next. After this degree bound is established, a Chebyshev-based polynomial blow-up lemma is applied: any polynomial of such low degree that vanishes at zero must deviate by a constant amount from the correct target somewhere on a sufficiently wide scaling interval. Choosing the scaling interval so that the corresponding covariance matrices range from αI to βI yields the lower bound L <= sqrt(β/α). For the unrestricted-attention case, the same scaling idea is applied to the entire hidden state Z instead of only the prediction coordinate. Another induction shows that each entry of Z at depth t is a polynomial in γ of degree at most 3^t. Applying the same polynomial blow-up lemma then implies that, unless 3^L is large enough, the network must incur constant error on some instance. Rewriting that condition in terms of the covariance range gives the logarithmic lower bound in the unrestricted setting.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem on_the_role_of_depth_and_looping_for_in_context_learning_with_task_diversity_theorem1
    {d : ℕ}
    {RestrictedWeights GeneralWeights : Type}
    (α β : ℝ)
    (L : ℕ)
    (xq : Fin d → ℝ)
    (InS : ℝ → ℝ → TDLRTInstance d → Prop)
    (SmallEnoughLogDepth : ℝ → ℝ → ℕ → Prop)
    (restrictedTF : RestrictedWeights → (Fin d → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (generalTF : GeneralWeights → (Fin d → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (restrictedWeights : RestrictedWeights)
    (generalWeights : GeneralWeights)
    (hα : 0 < α)
    (hαβ : α ≤ β)
    (hL : (L : ℝ) ≤ Real.sqrt (β / α))
    (hlog : SmallEnoughLogDepth α β L) :
    (∃ inst : TDLRTInstance d,
      InS α β inst ∧
      inst.query = xq ∧
      RestrictedRelativeErrorAtLeastHalf restrictedTF restrictedWeights inst) ∧
    GeneralCaseConstantError InS generalTF generalWeights α β xq := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

noncomputable section

structure TDLRTInstance (d : ℕ) where
  query : Fin d → ℝ
  target : ℝ

def RestrictedRelativeErrorAtLeastHalf
    {d : ℕ} {RestrictedWeights : Type}
    (restrictedTF : RestrictedWeights → (Fin d → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (weights : RestrictedWeights)
    (inst : TDLRTInstance d) : Prop :=
  inst.target ≠ 0 ∧
    |restrictedTF weights inst.query - inst.target| / |inst.target| ≥ (1 : ℝ) / 2

def GeneralCaseConstantError
    {d : ℕ} {GeneralWeights : Type}
    (InS : ℝ → ℝ → TDLRTInstance d → Prop)
    (generalTF : GeneralWeights → (Fin d → ℝ) → ℝ)
    (weights : GeneralWeights)
    (α β : ℝ)
    (xq : Fin d → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∃ inst : TDLRTInstance d,
    InS α β inst ∧
    inst.query = xq ∧
    inst.target ≠ 0 ∧
    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
      |generalTF weights inst.query - inst.target| / |inst.target| ≥ c

metadata.source_paper: On the Existence of Hidden Subnetworks Within a Randomly Weighted Multi-Head Attention Mechanism

natural_language.core_claim: A randomly initialized multi-head self-attention mechanism with only logarithmically wider hidden dimensions already contains a binary-masked subnetwork that can approximate any target multi-head attention mechanism with high probability.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a standard masked multi-head self-attention mechanism on a sequence of T input tokens, where the token embeddings form a matrix with input dimension d1, output dimension d2, each token embedding has norm at most α, and each attention mask attends to at least one token. Let the target mechanism have H heads, key dimension dK, value dimension dV, and per-head query, key, value, and output matrices whose operator norms are all at most one. Let the source mechanism have the same number of heads but source key and value dimensions nK and nV, and let its query and key entries be initialized independently and uniformly in the interval from minus nK to the power negative one quarter to plus nK to the power negative one quarter, while its value and output entries are initialized independently and uniformly in the interval from minus one to one. If α is at least the maximum of the square roots of d1 and d2, then there exists a universal positive constant C such that, whenever nK is at least d1 times C times the logarithm of eight H α cubed d1 to the three-halves divided by ε, and nV is at least d1 times C times the logarithm of two H α d1 square root of d2 divided by ε, the randomly initialized source mechanism contains, with probability at least one minus ε, a choice of binary masks on its query, key, value, and output matrices for which the maximum over all input tokens of the norm of the difference between the source and target attention outputs is at most ε.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument first rewrites each target head by merging its query and key matrices into a single query-key matrix, and likewise merging its value and output matrices into a single value-output matrix. This turns the attention-approximation problem into approximating two merged linear maps. It then applies a subset-sum-based approximation lemma to show that, when the source hidden dimensions are large enough, one can choose binary masks so that the products of the pruned random source query-key factors and the pruned random source value-output factors approximate the corresponding merged target matrices. Next, it analyzes how the query-key approximation error propagates through the masked softmax by proving a perturbation bound that is linear in the perturbation magnitude and, crucially, does not grow with the sequence length. Finally, it combines the softmax-side error and the value-output-side error across all heads, and uses a union bound to conclude that the pruned source attention approximates the target attention within ε with probability at least one minus ε.

formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open MeasureTheory
formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem existence_of_hidden_subnetworks_within_random_mha
    {Ω MaskSet : Type*}
    [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    {μ : Measure Ω}
    [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    {T d1 d2 H nK nV : ℕ}
    {α ε C : ℝ}
    (AttnT : Fin T → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d2))
    (AttnS : Ω → MaskSet → Fin T → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d2))
    -- each target-head query, key, value, and output matrix has operator norm at most 1
    (hTargetWeightsBounded : Prop)
    -- source query/key entries are i.i.d. uniform on [-nK^(-1/4), nK^(-1/4)]
    -- and source value/output entries are i.i.d. uniform on [-1, 1]
    (hSourceInitialization : Prop)
    (hε : 0 < ε ∧ ε < 1)
    (hα : α ≥ max (Real.sqrt (d1 : ℝ)) (Real.sqrt (d2 : ℝ)))
    (hC : 0 < C)
    (hk :
      (nK : ℝ) ≥
        (d1 : ℝ) * C *
          Real.log (8 * (H : ℝ) * α ^ (3 : ℕ) * ((d1 : ℝ) * Real.sqrt (d1 : ℝ)) / ε))
    (hv :
      (nV : ℝ) ≥
        (d1 : ℝ) * C *
          Real.log (2 * (H : ℝ) * α * (d1 : ℝ) * Real.sqrt (d2 : ℝ) / ε)) :
    μ {ω | ∃ M : MaskSet, ∀ i : Fin T, ‖AttnS ω M i - AttnT i‖ ≤ ε} ≥ ENNReal.ofReal (1 - ε) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Linear Attention for Efficient Bidirectional Sequence Modeling

natural_language.core_claim: Full bidirectional linear attention with decay-based masking admits an exactly equivalent bidirectional recurrent formulation, so its outputs can be computed through forward and backward linear state updates instead of quadratic attention.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider bidirectional full linear attention on a sequence, where each token has a query vector, a key vector, a value vector, and a scalar decay factor, and where the attention matrix is row-normalized. Then the same output can be written through two recurrent branches, one forward and one backward. In each branch, the matrix state at position i is updated by multiplying the previous state by the decay factor at position i and then adding the outer product of the current key and value, and the vector normalization state at position i is updated by multiplying the previous normalization state by the same decay factor and then adding the current key. The scalar normalization term for each branch is the inner product of the current query with that branch's normalization state minus one half of the current query-key inner product, and the vector output term for each branch is the current query applied to that branch's matrix state minus one half of the current query-key inner product times the current value. The final output at position i is obtained by dividing the sum of the forward and backward output terms by the sum of the forward and backward normalization terms.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument starts from the matrix form of bidirectional full linear attention and splits both the attention matrix and the bidirectional decay mask into forward and backward triangular parts, with the diagonal shared evenly between the two sides. This makes the row-normalized attention output decompose into a sum of a forward contribution and a backward contribution, together with a matching decomposition of the normalization denominator. The forward term is immediately recognized as the usual linear-attention recurrent computation. The backward term is then rewritten by reversing the token order, which shows that it has the same recurrent form when run in the opposite direction. After these two recurrent branches are written explicitly, the diagonal contribution appears in both branches, so one half of the self-interaction term is subtracted from each branch in both the numerator and denominator to avoid double counting. Combining the corrected forward and backward numerators and denominators yields exactly the stated output formula.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem laebsm_472581
    (L d : ℕ)
    (q k v : ℕ → Vec d)
    (decay : ℕ → ℝ) :
    ∃ SF SB : ℕ → Mat d,
      ∃ zF zB : ℕ → Vec d,
        ∃ cF cB : ℕ → ℝ,
          ∃ yF yB y : ℕ → Vec d,
            SF 0 = 0 ∧
            SB 0 = 0 ∧
            zF 0 = 0 ∧
            zB 0 = 0 ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              SF i = decay i • SF (i - 1) + outer (k i) (v i)) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              SB i = decay i • SB (i - 1) + outer (k i) (v i)) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              zF i = decay i • zF (i - 1) + k i) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              zB i = decay i • zB (i - 1) + k i) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              cF i = dot (q i) (zF i) - (dot (q i) (k i)) / 2) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              cB i = dot (q i) (zB i) - (dot (q i) (k i)) / 2) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              yF i = leftMulVec (q i) (SF i) - ((dot (q i) (k i)) / 2) • v i) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              yB i = leftMulVec (q i) (SB i) - ((dot (q i) (k i)) / 2) • v i) ∧
            (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 L,
              y i = (1 / (cF i + cB i)) • (yF i + yB i)) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators
open Matrix

noncomputable section

abbrev Vec (d : ℕ) := Fin d → ℝ
abbrev Mat (d : ℕ) := Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ

def dot {d : ℕ} (x y : Vec d) : ℝ :=
  ∑ i : Fin d, x i * y i

def outer {d : ℕ} (x y : Vec d) : Mat d :=
  fun i j => x i * y j

def leftMulVec {d : ℕ} (x : Vec d) (A : Mat d) : Vec d :=
  fun j => ∑ i : Fin d, x i * A i j

metadata.source_paper: DeltaFormer: Unlock the state space of Transformer

natural_language.core_claim: A single-layer DeltaFormer can exactly realize any finite sequence of pairwise swaps over n objects using only logarithmic head dimension, and the swap history can be compressed into an O(n log n) cache without changing the final answer.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let the list be the ordered sequence containing the n objects in their initial positions. For each pair of positions with the first strictly smaller than the second, define the corresponding swap operation to exchange the two objects at those positions and leave every other position unchanged. Then for every finite sequence of such swaps, there exists a single-layer DeltaFormer with head dimension on the order of the logarithm of n such that, given the swap sequence and a queried position, it returns exactly the object occupying that position after all swaps have been applied. Moreover, there exists a cache-compression operator that stores the entire swap sequence in a cache of size on the order of n times the logarithm of n, independent of the sequence length, together with another single-layer DeltaFormer that produces the same output from the compressed cache and the queried position.
natural_language.informal_proof: The construction begins by assigning almost orthogonal state keys so that inner products between distinct keys are uniformly small, and by choosing a decoding function that exactly recovers the integers minus one, zero, one, and two from small perturbations around those values. An auxiliary lemma then shows that when a new key is formed as the difference of two stored keys, applying the decoding function to its inner products is exactly the same as subtracting the two corresponding readout patterns. With this identity in hand, the first exchange step is handled directly: setting the new key to the difference of the two exchanged keys and setting the new value to zero makes the update vector equal to the difference between the two stored values, so querying either exchanged key returns the other value while every untouched key still returns its own value. The general case follows by induction. Assuming all earlier exchanges have been tracked correctly, the same algebra shows that the next update vector again becomes the difference between the two currently stored values that should be exchanged, and the readout equations therefore swap exactly those two values while preserving all others. This proves that the construction can trace an arbitrary sequence of exchanges, and the rephrased form packages this behavior as a single-layer DeltaFormer with logarithmic head dimension and an equivalent compressed-cache realization.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem deltaformer_unlock_state_space_transformer_381527
    (n : ℕ)
    (hn : 5 ≤ n)
    (IsSingleLayerDeltaFormerSeq : ℕ → (List (Fin n × Fin n) → Fin n → Fin n) → Prop)
    (IsSingleLayerDeltaFormerCache : ℕ → (List (Fin n) → Fin n → Fin n) → Prop) :
    ∃ C1 C2 headDim cacheSize : ℕ,
      0 < C1 ∧
      0 < C2 ∧
      headDim ≤ C1 * (Nat.log2 (n + 1) + 1) ∧
      cacheSize ≤ C2 * n * (Nat.log2 (n + 1) + 1) ∧
      ∃ F : List (Fin n × Fin n) → Fin n → Fin n,
        IsSingleLayerDeltaFormerSeq headDim F ∧
        (∀ gs : List (Fin n × Fin n), ∀ j : Fin n,
          (∀ s ∈ gs, s.1 < s.2) →
          F gs j =
            List.foldr
              (fun s pos =>
                if pos = s.1 then s.2
                else if pos = s.2 then s.1
                else pos)
              j
              gs) ∧
      ∃ ku : List (Fin n × Fin n) → List (Fin n),
        (∀ gs, (ku gs).length ≤ cacheSize) ∧
        ∃ H : List (Fin n) → Fin n → Fin n,
          IsSingleLayerDeltaFormerCache headDim H ∧
          ∀ gs : List (Fin n × Fin n), ∀ j : Fin n,
            (∀ s ∈ gs, s.1 < s.2) →
            H (ku gs) j = F gs j := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Dynamical Properties of Tokens in Self-Attention and Effects of Positional Encoding

natural_language.core_claim: In continuous-time self-attention dynamics, if the effective interaction matrix is negative definite and the score matrix is positive definite, then every token trajectory collapses to the origin.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the continuous-time self-attention dynamics for tokens x_1(t), ..., x_L(t) in a D-dimensional real space, where each token evolves according to the value-matrix transpose applied to the softmax-weighted average of the current tokens, and where the attention weights are computed from the bilinear scores x_l(t)^T W x_i(t). Assume the value matrix is invertible and define A to be W times the inverse transpose of the value matrix. If A is negative definite and W is positive definite, then every token converges to the zero vector as time tends to infinity.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument proceeds in three steps. First, the quadratic form associated with A is controlled using a differential inequality: Proposition 3.3 shows that along each token trajectory, the derivative of q_A(x_l(t)) is bounded from below by a term involving q_W(x_l(t)). Under the assumptions that A is negative definite and W has positive-definite symmetric part, this yields a uniform bound on all token trajectories. Second, Lemma B.4 uses an auxiliary energy function built from the pairwise exponentiated attention scores to prove that the squared A-norm of each time derivative is integrable on the half-line, and therefore every token velocity tends to zero. Third, assume for contradiction that the trajectories do not converge to zero. By boundedness, one can extract a subsequence of times converging to infinity along which the token configuration converges to a nonzero limit configuration. Passing to the limit in the dynamical system and using that the velocities vanish along this subsequence gives a stationary weighted-average identity for the limiting configuration. Lemma B.5 shows that, when W is definite, the only configuration satisfying that identity is the all-zero configuration. This contradicts the assumption that the limit configuration is nonzero, so the full trajectory must converge to zero.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem dptsaepe_472381
    {L D : ℕ}
    (hL : 0 < L)
    (hD : 0 < D)
    (W V A : Matrix (Fin D) (Fin D) ℝ)
    (x : ℝ → Fin L → Fin D → ℝ)
    (x0 : Fin L → Fin D → ℝ)
    (hA : A = W * (V.transpose)⁻¹)
    (hAneg : ∀ u : Fin D → ℝ, u ≠ 0 → dotProduct u (A.mulVec u) < 0)
    (hWpos : ∀ u : Fin D → ℝ, u ≠ 0 → 0 < dotProduct u (W.mulVec u))
    (hsol : IsSelfAttentionSolution W V x x0) :
    ∀ l : Fin L, Tendsto (fun t : ℝ => x t l) atTop (𝓝 (0 : Fin D → ℝ)) := by
  sorry
formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators Topology
open Filter

noncomputable section

def attentionWeight
    {L D : ℕ}
    (W : Matrix (Fin D) (Fin D) ℝ)
    (xt : Fin L → Fin D → ℝ)
    (l i : Fin L) : ℝ :=
  Real.exp (dotProduct (xt l) (W.mulVec (xt i))) /
    (∑ j : Fin L, Real.exp (dotProduct (xt l) (W.mulVec (xt j))))

def selfAttentionRHS
    {L D : ℕ}
    (W V : Matrix (Fin D) (Fin D) ℝ)
    (xt : Fin L → Fin D → ℝ)
    (l : Fin L) : Fin D → ℝ :=
  V.transpose.mulVec (∑ i : Fin L, attentionWeight W xt l i • xt i)

def IsSelfAttentionSolution
    {L D : ℕ}
    (W V : Matrix (Fin D) (Fin D) ℝ)
    (x : ℝ → Fin L → Fin D → ℝ)
    (x0 : Fin L → Fin D → ℝ) : Prop :=
  (∀ l : Fin L, x 0 l = x0 l) ∧
    ∀ t : ℝ, 0 ≤ t → ∀ l : Fin L,
      HasDerivAt (fun s : ℝ => x s l) (selfAttentionRHS W V (x t) l) t

metadata.source_paper: On the Role of Hidden States of Modern Hopfield Network in Transformer

natural_language.core_claim: Keeping a nonzero hidden-state carry term in modern Hopfield attention weakens the depth-wise residual decay from the double-exponential collapse of plain attention-only networks, thereby mitigating rank collapse in deep Transformers.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider an attention-only network of L layers with no skip connection, and measure residual size with the composite operator norm norm-one-infinity. If the hidden-state averaging coefficient alpha-prime is kept nonzero, then the residual of the L-layer network is bounded by the maximum, over integers m from zero to L, of the following product: r times one minus alpha-prime times C-one raised to the power one half times three-to-the-m minus one; r times alpha-prime times C-two raised to the power three-to-the-m times L minus m; and the residual norm of the input raised to the power three-to-the-m. Consequently, the resulting upper bound avoids the exponential decay that appears in the corresponding plain attention-only network.
natural_language.informal_proof: The argument begins by introducing a nonzero hidden state and proving a one-layer residual estimate for modern Hopfield attention. That one-layer estimate bounds the new residual by the maximum of two contributions: a cubic-decay term proportional to one minus alpha-prime, and a linear term proportional to alpha-prime. The crucial new ingredient is the linear term created by the hidden state, because it counteracts the pure third-order decay found in plain self-attention. The next step is to apply this one-layer inequality repeatedly across L layers. This generates a family of mixed terms indexed by how many times the cubic branch is taken, which produces exactly the maximum-form upper bound in the theorem. Finally, comparing these terms shows that the branch with m equal to zero yields a term of the form r times alpha-prime times C-two to the Lth power times the input residual norm, so the residual is no longer forced into the original double-exponential decay regime.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem rhsmhnt_482631
    {X : Type*}
    (AttnNet : X → X)
    (resNorm : X → ℝ)
    (x : X)
    (L : ℕ)
    (alpha alphaPrime r C1 C2 : ℝ)
    (h_alpha : alpha = 0)
    (h_alphaPrime_nonzero : alphaPrime ≠ 0) :
    let boundTerms : Finset ℝ :=
      (Finset.range (L + 1)).image (fun m : ℕ =>
        Real.rpow (r * (1 - alphaPrime) * C1) ((((3 : ℝ) ^ m) - 1) / 2) *
        Real.rpow (r * alphaPrime * C2) (((3 : ℝ) ^ m) * ↑(L - m)) *
        Real.rpow (resNorm x) ((3 : ℝ) ^ m))
    resNorm (AttnNet x) ≤
      boundTerms.max' (by
        classical
        have h_nonempty : (Finset.range (L + 1)).Nonempty := by
          exact Finset.nonempty_range_add_one
        exact h_nonempty.image (fun m : ℕ =>
          Real.rpow (r * (1 - alphaPrime) * C1) ((((3 : ℝ) ^ m) - 1) / 2) *
          Real.rpow (r * alphaPrime * C2) (((3 : ℝ) ^ m) * ↑(L - m)) *
          Real.rpow (resNorm x) ((3 : ℝ) ^ m))) := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Ehrenfeucht-Haussler Rank and Chain of Thought

natural_language.core_claim: The rank of a function is exactly the minimum number of chain-of-thought steps required by a single-layer decoder with one hard-attention head to compute it.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let n be a natural number, let Σ1, ..., Σn and O be finite sets, and let f be a function from Σ1 × ⋯ × Σn to O. Define the rank of f as the minimum depth of a decision tree over assignment queries that computes f, and define the one-head decoder depth of f as the minimum number of iterations needed by a single-layer decoder with one hard-attention head to compute f. Then the rank of f is equal to its one-head decoder depth.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof establishes both inequalities. For the direction from decoder depth to rank, assume that a single-head decoder computes the function in r iterations. After the first t outputs of the decoder are known, the next output can be recovered with one assignment query by ordering assignments according to the current attention score, with ties broken by the leftmost position, and querying the first assignment consistent with the input. Repeating this for r steps yields the final decoder output, which determines the function value, so the rank is at most r. For the direction from rank to decoder depth, start from an r-depth decision tree over assignment queries and construct a one-head decoder that simulates the tree. The decoder state after t iterations is designed to be the one-hot encoding of the depth-t tree node reached by the input. Positional coordinates encode the permutations used by the tree queries, the attention mechanism selects exactly the assignment returned by the current node's query, and a feed-forward map transforms the current node together with that selected assignment into the unique child reached by the corresponding edge. After r iterations, the decoder encodes the leaf reached by the tree, and that leaf determines the function value. Hence the one-head decoder depth is at most the rank.

formal_language.src_header:
import Mathlib

universe u v

abbrev EHRCOTInput {n : ℕ} (Alphabet : Fin n → Type u) := (i : Fin n) → Alphabet i

metadata.source_paper: Normalization in Attention Dynamics

natural_language.core_claim: Under generic random initialization, normalized attention dynamics asymptotically synchronize tokens into a single cluster across the normalization schemes studied, except that for Pre-LN, Mix-LN, and Peri-LN the only remaining alternative is asymptotically vanishing radial growth for some token.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider the normalized attention dynamics with query, key, and value matrices all equal to the identity. If the initial token directions are sampled independently and uniformly from the unit sphere, then under Post-LN, nGPT, and LN-Scaling the dynamics cluster asymptotically with probability one, meaning that the tokens synchronize to a single cluster. If instead the initial token embeddings are sampled in the form X(0) = r(0) · Θ(0), where the directions Θ(0) lie on the unit sphere and the magnitudes r(0) are real-valued, according to the standard Gaussian initialization considered in the theorem, then under Pre-LN, Mix-LN, and Peri-LN, with probability one either the token directions synchronize to a single cluster or the lower limit of the radial growth rate becomes zero for at least one token.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof first rewrites each normalization rule in a unified normalized-attention ODE and then performs a time change so that the resulting system is studied in a compact autonomous frame, which is especially important for schemes whose magnitudes can grow without bound. On the event that the rescaled speed factors stay uniformly positive, the angular dynamics become a generalized gradient-descent flow for an analytic energy, so a Łojasiewicz-type convergence argument shows that every trajectory converges to a critical point. The remaining task is to rule out non-synchronized limiting configurations. This is done by analyzing the Jacobian at a critical point: the angular block is a positive diagonal matrix times the Hessian of the energy, and the Hessian is unstable at every non-synchronized critical point, so the whole Jacobian is unstable there as well. Standard center-stable manifold arguments then imply that the basin of attraction of such unstable critical points has measure zero. Therefore, almost every trajectory either converges to the synchronized one-cluster state or, in the Pre-LN, Mix-LN, and Peri-LN setting, leaves the positivity regime through asymptotically vanishing radial growth, which yields exactly the stated dichotomy.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem NIAD_472581
    {Ω : Type*}
    [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    (μ : Measure Ω)
    {d : ℕ}
    (Q K V : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) ℝ)
    (hQ : Q = 1)
    (hK : K = 1)
    (hV : V = 1)
    (uniform_sphere_sample : Set Ω)
    (standard_gaussian_sample : Set Ω)
    (postln_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster : Set Ω)
    (ngpt_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster : Set Ω)
    (lnscaling_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster : Set Ω)
    (preln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero : Set Ω)
    (mixln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero : Set Ω)
    (periln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero : Set Ω) :
    μ (Set.diff uniform_sphere_sample
      (postln_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster ∩
        ngpt_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster ∩
        lnscaling_tokens_synchronize_to_one_cluster)) = 0 ∧
      μ (Set.diff standard_gaussian_sample
        (preln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero ∩
          mixln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero ∩
          periln_directions_synchronize_to_one_cluster_or_some_radial_growth_liminf_is_zero)) = 0 := by
  sorry

metadata.source_paper: Learning In-context n-grams with Transformers: Sub-n-grams Are Near-Stationary Points

natural_language.core_claim: The population cross-entropy gradient in in-context next-token prediction is the expected pairing between the model’s prediction residual and its score function.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider an in-context next-token predictor p_θ that maps a length-T sequence x^T of states to a probability distribution over the next state, and let p_τ(. | x^T) denote the true next-token distribution for a sampled context p_τ. For any parameter coordinate θ_i, the derivative of the population cross-entropy loss L(θ) with respect to θ_i equals the expectation, over p_τ sampled from the prior and x^T sampled from p_τ, of the inner product between the prediction residual p_θ(x^T) - p_τ(. | x^T) and the score vector ∂_{θ_i} log p_θ(x^T), where the logarithm is taken componentwise.
natural_language.informal_proof: Write the population loss as the expectation of the cross-entropy between the true next-token distribution and the model prediction. Differentiate inside the expectation and then inside the finite sum over next-token states. This produces a term involving -p_τ(. | x^T) paired with the componentwise derivative of log p_θ(x^T). Next, use probability normalization: the model output sums to 1, so differentiating that identity gives that the model distribution itself is orthogonal to the score vector in the required sense. Adding this zero term rewrites the derivative as the inner product between the residual p_θ(x^T) - p_τ(. | x^T) and the score vector. Averaging over the prior on contexts and over sampled sequences yields the stated formula.

theorem linwtsnansp_483271
    {Ctx Seq Tok Param : Type}
    [Fintype Ctx] [DecidableEq Ctx]
    [Fintype Seq] [DecidableEq Seq]
    [Fintype Tok] [DecidableEq Tok]
    [Fintype Param] [DecidableEq Param]
    (prior : Ctx → ℝ)
    (seqProb : Ctx → Seq → ℝ)
    (pred : Seq → Tok → ℝ)
    (trueCond : Ctx → Seq → Tok → ℝ)
    (score : Seq → Param → Tok → ℝ)
    (gradL : Param → ℝ) :
    ∀ i : Param,
      gradL i =
        ∑ τ, prior τ *
          ∑ x, seqProb τ x *
            ∑ y, (pred x y - trueCond τ x y) * score x i y := by

metadata.source_paper: The Expressivity of Fixed-Precision Transformers without Positional Encoding

natural_language.core_claim: Under fixed-precision arithmetic, no positional encoding, and the infinity-freeness condition on query-key products, Transformer decoders recognize exactly the finite and co-finite languages.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider Transformer decoders without positional encoding, operating at a fixed precision, and satisfying the infinity-freeness condition that in every attention layer, for all hidden vectors, the dot product between the query of one vector and the key of another is never plus or minus infinity. Then the class of languages recognized by such decoders is exactly the class of finite and co-finite languages. More explicitly, for every choice of precision, every decoding schedule that belongs to Omega of one, every parameter setting, and every set of accept-state tokens, the recognized language is finite or co-finite. Conversely, for every finite or co-finite language, there exist a choice of precision, a decoding schedule that belongs to Omega of one, a parameter setting, and a set of accept-state tokens such that the decoder recognizes exactly that language.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof has two parts. For the upper bound, the key step is a saturation argument in fixed precision. Under the infinity-freeness condition, every attention score remains finite, so when the input is long enough, the softmax denominator in causal attention grows until it saturates to positive infinity in fixed precision. This yields a threshold length with the property that any two sufficiently long inputs with the same final symbol produce the same decoder output. As a result, the model can only distinguish finitely many short strings, together with finitely many long-string cases determined by the last symbol, so the recognized language must be finite or co-finite. For the lower bound, the construction is explicit. A two-layer decoder first builds a representation that aggregates position-scaled token embeddings and thereby preserves enough information about the whole input string. A final feed-forward network then maps that representation to acceptance exactly for the target finite language. Co-finite languages are obtained by reversing the final decision on all but finitely many exceptional strings. Therefore every finite or co-finite language is recognized by some fixed-precision decoder in constant decoding steps.

theorem efptwpe_482731
    {Token Params : Type}
    (langOfNoPE : ℕ → (ℕ → ℕ) → Params → Set Token → Set (List Token))
    (InOmegaOne : (ℕ → ℕ) → Prop)
    (infinityFree : Params → Prop) :
    ((∀ (p : ℕ) (t : ℕ → ℕ) (θ : Params) (F : Set Token),
        InOmegaOne t →
        infinityFree θ →
        FiniteOrCofinite (langOfNoPE p t θ F)) ∧
     (∀ L : Set (List Token),
        FiniteOrCofinite L →
        ∃ (p : ℕ) (t : ℕ → ℕ) (θ : Params) (F : Set Token),
          InOmegaOne t ∧ infinityFree θ ∧ langOfNoPE p t θ F = L)) := by
  sorry

import Mathlib

open Set

def FiniteOrCofinite {α : Type} (L : Set (List α)) : Prop :=
  Set.Finite L ∨ Set.Finite Lᶜ

metadata.source_paper: MVA: Linear Attention with High-order Query-Keys Integration and Multi-level Vocabulary Decomposition

natural_language.core_claim: Recursive multi-level vocabulary decomposition makes the compression error contract multiplicatively across levels, which implies exponential growth of effective memory capacity.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Consider a recursive compression procedure carried out for c recursions, with final expected error E and original key sequence K. Then the norm of the final error is bounded by the norm of K multiplied by the product of the factors epsilon_i over all recursion levels and the product of the factors gamma_i over all recursion levels, where epsilon_i equals (n - m) / n and gamma_i is determined by learnable parameters chosen so that it converges to a value smaller than one.
natural_language.informal_proof: The proof analyzes the GSA-like branch of the model after removing the phi(Q) branch and treating the decay matrix as a learnable relative positional encoding. In that form, the output can be rewritten as standard masked Softmax attention followed by a dynamic low-rank matrix of the form F-hat(Q, K) W_S F(K)^T. The approximation error is therefore controlled by how close this low-rank matrix is to the identity. The argument then studies the compression map F and shows that using extremal mappings makes the rows of the relevant matrices approach aligned one-hot-like patterns, which reduces the mismatch with the identity matrix. The remaining error is decomposed recursively across levels, and each recursion contributes one multiplicative shrinkage factor from the compression ratio and one multiplicative shrinkage factor from learnable convergence parameters. Multiplying these levelwise reductions over c recursions yields the stated bound on the final error norm.

metadata.source_paper: Tensor Product Attention Is All You Need

natural_language.core_claim: Tensor Product Attention is fully compatible with rotary positional embeddings: applying RoPE to factorized query and key slices preserves the standard relative-position identity.
natural_language.informal_theorem: Let the query slice of token t be factorized as Q_t = (1 / R_Q) A_Q(x_t)^⊤ B_Q(x_t) in ℝ^(h × d_h), where A_Q(x_t) is in ℝ^(R_Q × h) and B_Q(x_t) is in ℝ^(R_Q × d_h). Then applying RoPE at position t gives RoPE_t(Q_t) = Q_t T_t = (1 / R_Q) A_Q(x_t)^⊤ B̃_Q(x_t), where B̃_Q(x_t) := B_Q(x_t) T_t = RoPE_t(B_Q(x_t)), with RoPE applied row-wise to B_Q(x_t). Furthermore, let Q̃_t = RoPE_t(Q_t) = Q_t T_t and K̃_s = RoPE_s(K_s) = K_s T_s be the RoPE-transformed query and key slices. Then the standard relative-position identity of RoPE is preserved: Q̃_t K̃_s^⊤ = Q_t T_(t-s) K_s^⊤, equivalently RoPE_(t-s)(Q_t) K_s^⊤ = Q̃_t K̃_s^⊤, where T_(t-s) := T_t T_s^⊤. In particular, for any head i, if q_(t,i), k_(s,i) are in ℝ^(1 × d_h) and q̃_(t,i) = q_(t,i) T_t, k̃_(s,i) = k_(s,i) T_s, then q̃_(t,i) k̃_(s,i)^⊤ = q_(t,i) T_(t-s) k_(s,i)^⊤.
natural_language.informal_proof: Write RoPE as post-multiplication by the block-diagonal rotation matrix T_t, so RoPE_t(Q_t) = Q_t T_t. Substitute the TPA factorization of Q_t and absorb T_t into the second factor, defining B̃_Q(x_t) = B_Q(x_t) T_t; this gives the factorized expression for the rotated query. Apply the same step to K_s, obtaining K̃_s = K_s T_s. Then expand Q̃_t K̃_s^⊤ using the two factorizations, combine the two positional transforms into the relative transform T_t T_s^⊤ = T_(t-s), and regroup the terms to obtain Q_t T_(t-s) K_s^⊤. The final head-wise identity is just the row-wise specialization of this matrix equality.

formal_language.formal_theorem:
theorem tensor_product_attention_rope_compatibility_481263
    {RQ RK H D : Type*}
    [Fintype RQ] [Fintype RK] [Fintype H] [Fintype D]
    [DecidableEq RQ] [DecidableEq RK] [DecidableEq H] [DecidableEq D]
    (AQ : Matrix RQ H ℝ)
    (BQ : Matrix RQ D ℝ)
    (AK : Matrix RK H ℝ)
    (BK : Matrix RK D ℝ)
    (Tt Ts : Matrix D D ℝ) :
    let Qt : Matrix H D ℝ := ((1 : ℝ) / (Fintype.card RQ : ℝ)) • (AQᵀ * BQ)
    let Ks : Matrix H D ℝ := ((1 : ℝ) / (Fintype.card RK : ℝ)) • (AKᵀ * BK)
    let BQtilde : Matrix RQ D ℝ := BQ * Tt
    let Qtilde : Matrix H D ℝ := Qt * Tt
    let Ktilde : Matrix H D ℝ := Ks * Ts
    let Tts : Matrix D D ℝ := Tt * Tsᵀ
    Qtilde = ((1 : ℝ) / (Fintype.card RQ : ℝ)) • (AQᵀ * BQtilde) ∧
      Qtilde * Ktildeᵀ = (Qt * Tts) * Ksᵀ ∧
      ∀ i : H, (Qtilde * Ktildeᵀ) i i = ((Qt * Tts) * Ksᵀ) i i := by
  sorry
